description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, styled as an 'opened' black box
teaching_value: Gives students a first structural decomposition of the Transformer, establishing the encoder-decoder paradigm before diving into details
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer lesson, immediately after introducing the model as a black box, to motivate the internal structure
suggested_caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with an input sentence in one language entering and a translated sentence exiting
teaching_value: Anchors the abstract architecture in a concrete use case (machine translation), helping students understand the input-output contract before examining internals
when_to_show: Show at the very beginning of a Transformer introduction to ground the architecture in a real-world task before opening the black box
suggested_caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translation out. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing a stack of six encoders on the left and a stack of six decoders on the right, with arrows indicating data flow and connections between the two stacks
teaching_value: Clarifies that both encoder and decoder are stacks of repeated identical-structure layers, and that the number six is a design choice, not a fixed rule
when_to_show: Show after introducing the black-box view, when explaining that the Transformer is composed of repeated encoder and decoder layers
suggested_caption: Six stacked encoders feed into six stacked decoders in the Transformer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with input flowing up through both
teaching_value: Isolates the internal structure of one encoder, making the two-sub-layer composition concrete and showing the sequential data flow from self-attention to feed-forward
when_to_show: Show when drilling into a single encoder block after establishing the stack structure, to explain what each encoder actually computes
suggested_caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and Feed-Forward Neural Network, with inputs from both the encoder stack and the previous decoder output
teaching_value: Shows how the decoder differs from the encoder by adding a cross-attention layer that attends to encoder outputs, making the encoder-decoder connection explicit
when_to_show: Show after explaining the encoder structure, when contrasting the decoder to highlight the additional cross-attention mechanism
suggested_caption: Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing input words being converted into embedding vectors (represented as colored columns of size 512) before entering the bottom encoder, with each word mapped to a fixed-size vector
teaching_value: Illustrates the word-to-vector conversion step and establishes the 512-dimensional embedding size as the standard input representation throughout the encoder stack
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from architecture overview to data flow, specifically when explaining how raw words become numerical inputs to the Transformer
suggested_caption: Input words converted to 512-dimensional embedding vectors before encoding. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors flowing in parallel through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers of an encoder, with each word following its own path
teaching_value: Visualizes the parallel processing property of the Transformer encoder and shows that each token has its own path, while hinting at inter-path dependencies in self-attention
when_to_show: Show when explaining how the encoder processes a full sequence, emphasizing parallelism as a key advantage over RNNs
suggested_caption: Each word vector flows its own path through encoder layers in parallel. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating the encoder processing a short example sentence, showing how each token's vector representation passes through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers with concrete word labels
teaching_value: Makes the abstract data-flow diagram concrete by attaching real words to the vectors, helping students trace how a specific sentence is encoded step by step
when_to_show: Show when walking through a worked example of encoding, after introducing the encoder structure, to connect theory to a concrete sentence
suggested_caption: Concrete sentence tokens flowing through encoder self-attention and feed-forward layers. (Alammar)

description: Attention visualization heatmap showing the word 'it' in a sentence attending strongly to 'animal', with colored lines or shading indicating attention weights between the query word and all other words
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, visual proof of what self-attention computes — that a pronoun can learn to refer back to its antecedent — making the abstract mechanism tangible
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention conceptually, before the mathematical details, to motivate why attention is useful for capturing word relationships
suggested_caption: Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', visualizing learned word relationships. (Alammar)

description: Step-by-step diagram showing how Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors are derived from input word embeddings by multiplying with learned weight matrices WQ, WK, WV, with vector dimensions labeled (64 vs 512)
teaching_value: Demystifies the Q/K/V abstraction by showing the exact linear transformation that produces each vector, and explains the dimensionality reduction from 512 to 64 as an architectural choice
when_to_show: Show when explaining the first step of self-attention computation, after motivating why attention is useful, to ground the Q/K/V formalism in concrete matrix operations
suggested_caption: Query, Key, Value vectors derived from embeddings via learned weight matrices. (Alammar)

description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the dot-product scoring mechanism, showing students exactly which vectors interact and why — making the abstract formula q·k tangible with labeled arrows and example words.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing Q/K/V vectors, when explaining step 2 of self-attention — before normalization or softmax are introduced.
suggested_caption: Step 2: Raw attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)

description: Diagram extending the scoring steps to include dividing raw scores by √64 (scaling) and applying softmax, producing normalized attention weights that sum to 1, shown alongside value vectors ready to be weighted.
teaching_value: Explains why scaling is necessary (gradient stability) and how softmax converts raw scores into a probability distribution, helping students understand both the mathematical motivation and the intuition of 'how much to attend' to each word.
when_to_show: Show after the raw scoring diagram (Image 1), when covering steps 3 and 4 of self-attention — use to motivate why we scale before softmax.
suggested_caption: Steps 3–4: Scale scores by √d_k, then apply softmax to normalize. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing steps 5 and 6 of self-attention: each value vector is multiplied by its corresponding softmax weight, then all weighted value vectors are summed to produce the final self-attention output vector z1 for the first word.
teaching_value: Closes the loop on the full self-attention computation, showing students how attention weights gate value vectors and how summation aggregates context — making the 'weighted sum of values' formula concrete and intuitive.
when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram (Image 2), when completing the step-by-step walkthrough of self-attention — ideal for summarizing the full single-head computation before moving to matrix form.
suggested_caption: Steps 5–6: Weighted value vectors summed to produce self-attention output z1. (Alammar)

description: Matrix-form diagram showing how input embeddings packed into matrix X are multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all words in the sequence.
teaching_value: Bridges the word-level intuition to the efficient matrix implementation, helping students understand how the computation parallelizes across all tokens simultaneously — critical for understanding why Transformers are fast.
when_to_show: Show after completing the step-by-step word-level walkthrough, when transitioning to the matrix formulation — emphasize the efficiency gain over sequential computation.
suggested_caption: Matrix form: X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V yields Q, K, V matrices. (Alammar)

description: Single formula diagram showing the complete self-attention computation condensed as: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, with the matrix operations visually laid out to show how steps 2–6 collapse into one expression.
teaching_value: Presents the canonical self-attention formula in a visual context, allowing students to map each matrix operation back to the conceptual steps they already learned — essential for reading research papers.
when_to_show: Show after the W^Q/W^K/W^V matrix diagram (Image 4), as the culminating formula — use to connect the visual steps to the equation seen in the original paper.
suggested_caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating multi-head attention by showing the same Q/K/V computation performed eight times in parallel with different weight matrices (W^Q_0…W^Q_7, etc.), producing eight separate sets of Q, K, V matrices and eight Z output matrices.
teaching_value: Makes the 'multiple heads' concept concrete by showing the replication of the weight matrix sets, helping students understand that each head independently learns different attention patterns rather than sharing parameters.
when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-head attention, after students understand single-head self-attention — use to motivate why multiple heads allow the model to attend to different positions simultaneously.
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: eight independent Q/K/V projections run in parallel. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the eight Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) produced by eight attention heads, illustrating the challenge that the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix rather than eight separate ones.
teaching_value: Sets up the problem that motivates the W^O projection step, helping students understand why concatenation and linear projection are necessary — connecting the multi-head design to practical architectural constraints.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head Q/K/V diagram (Image 6), to pose the 'how do we combine eight matrices?' problem before revealing the W^O solution.
suggested_caption: Eight attention heads produce eight Z matrices — must be combined for feed-forward layer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing how the eight Z matrices are concatenated into a single wide matrix and then multiplied by the learned weight matrix W^O to produce the final multi-head attention output matrix Z of the correct dimensionality.
teaching_value: Explains the final projection step of multi-head attention, showing students how learned linear combination of all heads' outputs allows the model to synthesize information across all representation subspaces into one output.
when_to_show: Show after the eight-Z-matrices diagram (Image 7), as the resolution to the dimensionality problem — emphasize that W^O is also trained and learns to combine head outputs meaningfully.
suggested_caption: Concatenated Z matrices projected by W^O to produce final attention output. (Alammar)

description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-head self-attention pipeline in one visual: input X, eight sets of W^Q/W^K/W^V weight matrices, eight parallel attention computations, concatenation, W^O projection, and final output Z.
teaching_value: Provides a unified overview of the complete multi-head attention mechanism, allowing students to see all components and their relationships simultaneously — ideal for consolidating understanding before moving to the next topic.
when_to_show: Show as a summary after walking through each individual step of multi-head attention — use as a reference diagram students can return to when reviewing the full mechanism.
suggested_caption: Complete multi-head self-attention pipeline from input X to output Z. (Alammar)

description: Attention visualization showing which tokens the word 'it' attends to across different attention heads when encoding the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to different words.
teaching_value: Provides an interpretable, real example of what attention heads learn, demonstrating that different heads capture different linguistic relationships (e.g., coreference resolution) — making the abstract benefit of multiple heads concrete.
when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-head attention mechanics, when discussing what attention heads learn in practice — use to motivate why multiple heads improve model capability with a concrete linguistic example.
suggested_caption: Attention heads show 'it' attending to 'animal' and 'street' — different heads, different focus. (Alammar)

description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to multiple other words, making the combined view cluttered and hard to interpret.
teaching_value: Demonstrates why visualizing all attention heads at once is cognitively overwhelming, motivating the need to examine heads individually and understand that each head captures different relationships.
when_to_show: After showing individual attention head visualizations, use this to illustrate the interpretability challenge of multi-head attention and prompt discussion about what each head might specialize in.
suggested_caption: All attention heads combined for 'it' — hard to interpret. (Alammar, The Illustrated Transformer)

description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder, with a 4-dimensional example illustrating how position-specific vectors are element-wise added to embeddings.
teaching_value: Concretely shows the mechanism by which positional information is injected into the model — a vector addition step — helping students understand that position is encoded in the input representation itself, not a separate input.
when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use this before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap to build intuition with a simple 4D example.
suggested_caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar)

description: Small concrete numerical example of positional encoding with 4-dimensional embeddings, showing the actual numeric values of positional encoding vectors for the first few positions in a sequence.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract concept of positional encoding in concrete numbers, allowing students to see exactly what values are added and how they differ across positions.
when_to_show: Use immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding to give students a tangible, low-dimensional example before scaling up to 512 dimensions.
suggested_caption: Concrete 4D positional encoding values per sequence position. (Alammar)

description: Color-coded heatmap grid where each row represents the 512-dimensional positional encoding vector for one word position (up to ~20 positions shown), with values ranging from -1 to 1 displayed as a color gradient, revealing a structured sinusoidal pattern.
teaching_value: Reveals the sinusoidal pattern of positional encodings visually, helping students understand that each position has a unique, structured encoding and that the pattern allows the model to generalize to unseen sequence lengths.
when_to_show: After the 4D example; use this to scale intuition to real model dimensions and discuss why the sinusoidal pattern enables length generalization.
suggested_caption: 512-dim positional encoding heatmap: each row is one word position. (Alammar)

description: Heatmap from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper showing positional encodings, likely displaying the interleaved sinusoidal pattern (alternating sine/cosine) across dimensions and positions, as referenced in the July 2020 update.
teaching_value: Shows the original paper's positional encoding visualization, allowing students to compare the Tensor2Tensor implementation (concatenated) with the paper's interleaved sine/cosine approach, deepening understanding of implementation variants.
when_to_show: When discussing the difference between the paper's positional encoding and the Tensor2Tensor implementation; useful for advanced students reading the original paper.
suggested_caption: Original paper's interleaved sinusoidal positional encoding pattern. (Vaswani et al., via Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection and layer normalization step around the self-attention sub-layer, with vectors flowing in, being processed, added back to the input, and then normalized.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates the Add & Norm pattern — a critical architectural detail — showing students how residual connections preserve gradient flow and how layer normalization stabilizes training.
when_to_show: When explaining encoder sub-layer architecture; use this to show that self-attention output is not used directly but combined with the original input before normalization.
suggested_caption: Residual connection and layer normalization around self-attention sub-layer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a stacked Transformer with 2 encoders and 2 decoders, showing residual connections and layer normalization around each sub-layer (self-attention and feed-forward) in both encoder and decoder stacks.
teaching_value: Shows how the Add & Norm pattern applies throughout the entire encoder-decoder stack, helping students see the full architecture and understand that residual connections are pervasive, not just in one layer.
when_to_show: After explaining residual connections in a single encoder; use this to show the full stacked architecture and reinforce that the pattern repeats at every sub-layer.
suggested_caption: 2-layer Transformer stack with residual connections and layer norm throughout. (Alammar)

description: Detailed vector-level diagram of the self-attention sub-layer with residual connection and layer normalization, showing input vectors x1/x2, the self-attention output z1/z2, the addition of residuals, and the layer-norm output fed to the feed-forward network.
teaching_value: Provides the most granular view of the Add & Norm operation at the vector level, making explicit that each token's representation is individually normalized after adding the residual, which is essential for understanding the data flow.
when_to_show: Use when students need to trace exactly how a single token's vector is transformed through one encoder sub-layer, especially when debugging understanding of shapes and operations.
suggested_caption: Vector-level view: self-attention with residual add and layer normalization. (Alammar)

description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer: the encoder processes the full input sequence, and the decoder begins generating the first output token, with attention vectors K and V passed from the top encoder to each decoder layer.
teaching_value: Animates the transition from encoding to decoding, making the sequential, autoregressive nature of decoding concrete and showing how encoder outputs (K, V) are reused at every decoder step.
when_to_show: When introducing the decoder; use this animation to show the first generation step and explain how encoder context is passed to the decoder via K and V vectors.
suggested_caption: Step 1 of decoding: encoder outputs K/V vectors used by decoder. (Alammar)

description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, illustrating how each generated token is fed back as input to the decoder at the next time step, with the decoder attending to both previous outputs and encoder K/V vectors.
teaching_value: Shows the full autoregressive loop in action — each output token becomes the next input — and illustrates masked self-attention (attending only to previous positions), helping students understand why decoding is sequential unlike encoding.
when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use this to show the full generation loop and explain masked self-attention and why future positions must be masked during training.
suggested_caption: Autoregressive decoding: each output token fed back as next decoder input. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how raw decoder outputs (logits) are transformed into a probability distribution over the vocabulary and how a single output word is selected, bridging abstract math to the actual word-generation mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how the Transformer produces its final word prediction — specifically when transitioning from internal representations to human-readable output tokens.
suggested_caption: Linear layer produces logits; Softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)

description: A one-hot encoding vector example for a small vocabulary, showing how the word 'am' is represented as a vector with a single 1 at the position corresponding to 'am' and 0s elsewhere.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of one-hot encoding tangible by showing exactly how a discrete vocabulary word maps to a fixed-length binary vector, which is foundational for understanding both input embeddings and output target labels.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how words are represented as vectors during training, especially before discussing loss functions that compare model output distributions to target one-hot vectors.
suggested_caption: One-hot encoding represents 'am' as a single-1 binary vector. (Alammar)

description: A one-hot encoded vector over a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), visually showing which position is active for a specific word, used to illustrate target labels in training.
teaching_value: Grounds the concept of one-hot encoding in a minimal, concrete vocabulary example, helping students see exactly how a target word becomes a training signal vector that the model's output distribution is compared against.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how training targets are formatted — particularly when setting up the loss function discussion and the 'merci' → 'thanks' translation example.
suggested_caption: Six-word vocabulary with one-hot target vector for training. (Alammar)

description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (logits/softmax output across vocabulary) versus the one-hot target label for the word 'thanks', illustrating how loss is computed by comparing the two distributions.
teaching_value: Directly visualizes the core training signal: the gap between what the model predicts and what the correct answer is, making cross-entropy loss intuitive before the formal definition is introduced.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function — use it to motivate why we need a metric to measure the difference between predicted and target distributions.
suggested_caption: Model output vs. target distribution — the basis of cross-entropy loss. (Alammar)

description: A sequence of probability distributions the model should ideally output at each decoding step for translating 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', showing one distribution per output token with the correct word having near-zero probability in an untrained model.
teaching_value: Illustrates the sequential nature of decoder output and what the training objective looks like across multiple time steps, helping students understand that the model must learn to produce correct distributions at every position in the output sequence.
when_to_show: Show when explaining multi-step training targets — after the single-word loss example, to scale up to realistic sentence-level training scenarios.
suggested_caption: Target probability distributions at each decoding step during training. (Alammar)

description: Probability distributions produced by a well-trained Transformer model at each decoding step for 'i am a student', showing high probability mass concentrated on the correct output word at each position, contrasting with the untrained model's distributions.
teaching_value: Shows what successful training looks like — the model's output distributions become sharply peaked at correct words — and sets up the discussion of greedy decoding vs. beam search by showing how a word is selected from each distribution.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the untrained/target distributions to contrast before-and-after training, and to introduce decoding strategies like greedy decoding and beam search.
suggested_caption: Trained model outputs near-perfect probability distributions per decoding step. (Alammar)

description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit, with colored bounding boxes highlighting specific regions: a yellow box on one ear and red boxes on the nose, other ear, and eyes, illustrating selective visual attention.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, concrete analogy for how attention mechanisms work — humans focus on high-resolution regions (yellow box) while perceiving surroundings at lower resolution, mirroring how neural attention weights certain inputs more heavily.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing attention mechanisms, before any math, to ground the abstract concept in human perceptual experience.
suggested_caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus (yellow) with low-res periphery (red).

description: A sentence with attention weights visualized as colored arcs or highlights showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths.
teaching_value: Bridges the visual attention analogy to linguistic attention, showing students that words in a sentence have differential relevance to each other — directly motivating self-attention and sequence-to-sequence attention.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention dog example, to transition from image-based intuition to NLP-based attention before introducing formal definitions.
suggested_caption: Word-level attention: each word attends differently to others in a sentence.

description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder RNN architecture translating 'she is eating a green apple' to Chinese, with the encoder unrolled left-to-right processing source tokens and the decoder unrolled producing target tokens, connected through a single fixed-length context vector.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the bottleneck problem of seq2seq models — all source information must be compressed into one fixed-size vector — making the motivation for attention mechanisms immediately clear.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention, so students understand the problem attention solves.
suggested_caption: Encoder-decoder seq2seq: all source info compressed into one context vector.

description: Diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention encoder-decoder architecture, showing the encoder hidden states connected via attention weights (alpha) to a context vector that feeds into each decoder step, with alignment score computation illustrated.
teaching_value: Shows exactly how attention resolves the bottleneck: instead of one fixed vector, each decoder step computes a weighted sum over all encoder hidden states, with the weights (alignments) learned dynamically. Makes the architectural change concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Bahdanau (2015) attention mechanism, after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck, to illustrate the architectural solution.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context vector from all encoder states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: A heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; brighter cells indicate higher attention weights, revealing near-diagonal alignment for most words and non-monotonic alignment for reordered phrases.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that attention learns linguistically meaningful alignments without supervision. Students can see how the model handles word reordering between languages, making abstract alignment scores tangible.
when_to_show: Show after explaining alignment scores mathematically, to validate that the learned weights correspond to intuitive word-level translations.
suggested_caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English translation weights. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and blue shading on previous words indicates how strongly each is attended to, showing long-range dependencies within a sentence.
teaching_value: Demonstrates self-attention in action on a real NLP task, showing students that a model can learn to relate a current word to relevant earlier context across long distances — something vanilla RNNs struggle with.
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention, to illustrate how a single sequence can attend to itself and why this is useful for capturing dependencies.
suggested_caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior context (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)

description: An image of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with overlaid attention weight heatmaps showing which image regions the model attends to when generating each word of the caption 'A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park.'
teaching_value: Illustrates soft attention applied to image captioning, showing students how attention weights over spatial image features correspond to semantically relevant regions for each generated word.
when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs hard attention, and when demonstrating attention applied to vision tasks beyond NLP.
suggested_caption: Soft attention for image captioning: attended regions per generated word. (Xu et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (all source hidden states contribute to context) and local attention (only a window of source states around a predicted position contribute), with alignment weight computation shown for each.
teaching_value: Clarifies the trade-off between global (soft) and local (hard/soft hybrid) attention: global is computationally expensive but fully differentiable; local reduces computation while remaining differentiable by focusing on a predicted window.
when_to_show: Show when teaching Luong et al. (2015) attention variants, after covering Bahdanau attention, to highlight design choices in attention mechanisms.
suggested_caption: Global vs local attention: full context vs windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)

description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine showing a controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.
teaching_value: Shows how attention mechanisms extend beyond sequence-to-sequence models to enable differentiable memory access in NTMs, helping students see attention as a general mechanism for soft, weighted selection over a set of stored representations.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Neural Turing Machines to illustrate how attention generalizes to memory-augmented neural networks beyond translation tasks.
suggested_caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller + attention-addressed external memory bank.

description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over discrete shifts and a wrapped Gaussian distribution over shift positions.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design choice in NTM location-based addressing — how a probability distribution over memory shifts can be parameterized, and the trade-offs between discrete softmax and continuous Gaussian approaches.
when_to_show: Show when explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically when students ask how the controller specifies which memory positions to attend to via convolution-like shifts.
suggested_caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting: softmax vs. wrapped Gaussian.

description: A detailed flow diagram showing the full addressing pipeline in a Neural Turing Machine: starting from the controller output, through content-based addressing (key/beta), interpolation gate (g_t), location-based shift convolution (s_t), and sharpening (gamma_t) to produce the final attention weight vector w_t.
teaching_value: Provides a complete visual walkthrough of how NTM attention is computed step-by-step, allowing students to trace each parameter's role and understand how content-based and location-based addressing are combined.
when_to_show: Show as a summary diagram after introducing each individual NTM addressing component, or when students need to see how all the pieces (key, beta, g_t, s_t, gamma_t) fit together into one pipeline.
suggested_caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content → interpolation → shift → sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence x1…xn and a decoder RNN producing outputs c1…cm, where attention scores over encoder states are used directly as pointers to input positions rather than generating new tokens.
teaching_value: Illustrates how Pointer Networks differ from standard seq-2-seq models by using attention weights as output probabilities over input positions, making the output vocabulary dynamic and input-size-dependent — key for understanding combinatorial problems like sorting.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Pointer Networks, especially when contrasting with standard NMT attention to highlight that here attention IS the output, not just a context vector.
suggested_caption: Pointer Network: attention weights directly select input positions as outputs. (Vinyals et al., 2015)

description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) the scaled dot-product attention block with Q, K, V inputs, MatMul, Scale, optional Mask, Softmax, and weighted sum; (right) the multi-head wrapper showing h parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.
teaching_value: Visually decomposes the two-level structure of multi-head attention — students can see both the inner mechanics of a single attention head and how multiple heads are run in parallel and merged, clarifying why multi-head attention captures diverse representation subspaces.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing the Transformer's attention mechanism; use the left sub-diagram to explain a single head, then the right to explain why multiple heads are used.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention: parallel heads concatenated and projected. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding fed into N repeated layers, each containing a multi-head self-attention sub-layer and a position-wise feed-forward sub-layer, both wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Shows students the modular structure of the Transformer encoder — how positional encoding, self-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, and layer norm are composed — enabling understanding of information flow through the encoder.
when_to_show: Show when teaching the Transformer encoder architecture, after explaining multi-head attention, to demonstrate how attention is embedded within a larger residual block structure.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residual connections. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding fed into N repeated layers containing masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers, all with residual connections and layer norm, followed by a linear + softmax output layer.
teaching_value: Helps students understand how the decoder differs from the encoder — specifically the addition of masked self-attention (to prevent future token leakage) and cross-attention over encoder outputs — and how the final output distribution is produced.
when_to_show: Show after the encoder diagram when explaining the full Transformer; emphasize the masking in self-attention and the cross-attention layer that connects encoder and decoder.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder: masked self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Full Transformer architecture diagram combining both encoder (left stack) and decoder (right stack) with all components: embeddings, positional encodings, multi-head attention layers, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer norm, and the final linear+softmax output, showing data flow between encoder and decoder.
teaching_value: Provides the complete picture of the Transformer model, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact end-to-end and consolidating all previously introduced components into one unified view.
when_to_show: Show as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or when students need to understand the full data flow from input tokens to output probability distribution.
suggested_caption: Complete Transformer architecture with encoder-decoder stacks. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Architecture diagram of the SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) model showing interleaved temporal convolutional layers (for aggregating context over time) and causal attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information), applied to a sequence of (input, label) pairs for meta-learning.
teaching_value: Illustrates how SNAIL combines two complementary mechanisms — dense temporal convolutions for broad context and attention for precise retrieval — solving the positional dependency weakness of pure attention models in RL/meta-learning settings.
when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of the Transformer's weak positional encoding and how SNAIL addresses this for reinforcement learning and meta-learning tasks.
suggested_caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)

description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (small local patch highlighted on a feature map) versus self-attention (entire feature map highlighted), illustrating the difference in spatial coverage between the two operations.
teaching_value: Gives students an immediate intuition for why self-attention is powerful in vision tasks — it can relate any two spatial positions directly, while convolutions are limited to local neighborhoods and require many layers to capture long-range dependencies.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the SAGAN section to motivate why self-attention is added to GANs — students should understand the local vs. global coverage limitation before seeing the SAGAN architecture.
suggested_caption: Convolution sees local patches; self-attention accesses all spatial positions globally.

description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing convolutional feature maps x branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f→key, g→query, h→value), with attention map beta computed via softmax over dot products, then used to weight value features and produce the attended output o added back via a learned scalar to the input.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how the key-query-value attention framework from NLP Transformers is adapted to 2D image feature maps using 1x1 convolutions, helping students transfer their understanding of attention to the vision domain.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the key-query-value framework in Transformers, to demonstrate how the same concept applies to spatial feature maps in image generation models.
suggested_caption: SAGAN self-attention: 1x1 conv branches produce keys, queries, values over spatial features. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A grid of 128×128 images generated by Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) across multiple ImageNet classes, showing high-quality synthesized images of animals, objects, and scenes with fine structural details.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical output quality achievable when self-attention mechanisms are incorporated into GANs, helping students connect the abstract attention formulation (attention maps, 1x1 convolutions, scale parameters) to tangible generative results with coherent long-range spatial structure.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h, and the residual scale output) to motivate why the added complexity matters — use it as a 'results payoff' moment.
suggested_caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention's role in coherent synthesis. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A diagram illustrating the attention mechanism: a query q is compared against multiple keys k_i using a compatibility function a(q,k), the resulting scores are passed through softmax to produce attention weights, and these weights are used to compute a weighted linear combination of values v_i to produce the output.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual model of how queries, keys, and values interact in attention pooling — making the abstract mathematical formulation tangible by showing the flow from inputs to weighted output.
when_to_show: Show this diagram immediately when introducing the QKV attention framework, before or alongside the softmax weight formula, to anchor the mathematical notation to a visual structure.
suggested_caption: Attention pooling: query-key compatibility scores weight value aggregation. (D2L.ai Fig. 11.1.1)

description: A heatmap visualization output from the show_heatmaps function, displaying attention weight matrices as color-coded grids where intensity represents the magnitude of attention weights assigned to different key positions for given queries.
teaching_value: Helps students develop intuition for attention weight distributions by visually showing which keys receive high vs. low attention for different queries, making the abstract concept of 'selection' concrete.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the show_heatmaps function and before discussing specific attention patterns, to help students understand how to read and interpret attention weight heatmaps.
suggested_caption: Heatmap of attention weights across queries and keys. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)

description: A heatmap visualization showing a specific attention weight pattern — likely a diagonal or identity-like pattern — produced by the show_heatmaps function, demonstrating how a model can learn to attend to specific key positions.
teaching_value: Illustrates a concrete example of attention selectivity, showing students how large weights concentrate on relevant keys, reinforcing the intuition that attention can act as a soft selection mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how attention weights can represent selective focus, particularly when contrasting uniform vs. peaked attention distributions.
suggested_caption: Attention heatmap showing selective key weighting for given queries. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)

description: A heatmap visualization output from show_heatmaps demonstrating another attention weight pattern, possibly showing a different query set or a different scoring function, to contrast with previous examples.
teaching_value: Provides a comparative example of attention weight distributions, helping students see how different queries or scoring functions produce different attention patterns across the same set of keys.
when_to_show: Show alongside other heatmap examples when comparing the effect of different queries or attention functions, to build intuition through contrast.
suggested_caption: Comparative attention heatmap illustrating varied query-key weight patterns. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)

description: A heatmap visualization from show_heatmaps showing yet another attention weight configuration, likely demonstrating a specific property such as uniform attention, sharp attention, or the effect of a particular scoring function.
teaching_value: Extends the student's visual vocabulary for attention patterns, showing edge cases or special configurations (e.g., uniform weights) that help clarify the range of behaviors the attention mechanism can exhibit.
when_to_show: Show when summarizing the range of possible attention behaviors, or when discussing degenerate cases like uniform attention to contrast with focused attention.
suggested_caption: Attention weight heatmap demonstrating a distinct attention distribution pattern. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)

description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder RNN architecture for neural machine translation, showing the encoder reading source words up to an <eos> token and the decoder emitting target words one at a time.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of how the encoder processes a full source sentence before the decoder begins generating output, establishing the baseline seq2seq architecture that attention mechanisms improve upon.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of the NMT lesson, before introducing attention, to establish the baseline encoder-decoder bottleneck problem.
suggested_caption: Encoder-decoder RNN: encoder reads source, decoder emits target words. (Source)

description: Mathematical formula showing the conditional probability of each target word yi conditioned on a distinct context vector ci, along with the alignment model eij that scores how well input position j matches output position i using hidden state si-1 and annotation hj.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students how Bahdanau attention replaces a single fixed context vector with a per-timestep context vector, and introduces the alignment score formula that drives the attention mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the fixed-context encoder-decoder to Bahdanau attention, to formalize the key mathematical difference.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau alignment model: each target word attends to a unique context vector.

description: Step-by-step procedural diagram of the full Bahdanau attention process, illustrating how a bidirectional RNN encoder produces annotations and how the decoder uses attention over those annotations to generate each target word.
teaching_value: Provides a high-level visual roadmap of the entire Bahdanau attention pipeline, helping students see how bidirectional encoding, annotation creation, and selective decoding fit together.
when_to_show: Show as an overview before diving into each individual step of the Bahdanau attention process, so students have a mental map of the full pipeline.
suggested_caption: Full Bahdanau attention pipeline: bidirectional encoder feeds selective decoder. (Source)

description: Diagram illustrating Phase 1 of the attention decoder: collecting all encoder hidden state outputs and pairing them with the current decoder hidden state to compute alignment scores at each time step.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the first concrete step inside the attention decoder — that all encoder outputs must be gathered before alignment scoring begins — grounding the abstract mechanism in a visual workflow.
when_to_show: Show when beginning the step-by-step walkthrough of the Bahdanau decoder, specifically at the 'collecting encoder outputs' phase.
suggested_caption: Step 1: Collect encoder outputs to compute alignment scores. (Source)

description: Diagram showing Phase 2 of Bahdanau attention: the decoder hidden state and encoder outputs each pass through separate Linear layers with trainable weights, are combined, and then passed through a tanh activation to produce intermediate alignment scores.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how the feedforward alignment network works — separate linear projections followed by tanh nonlinearity — making the mathematical formula tangible for students.
when_to_show: Show during the explanation of how alignment scores are computed, specifically to illustrate the role of linear projections and tanh in the scoring network.
suggested_caption: Step 2: Linear layers + tanh combine hidden state and encoder outputs. (Source)

description: Diagram illustrating Phase 3 of Bahdanau attention: applying softmax to the alignment score vector to produce attention weights, with each value between 0 and 1 representing the relative importance of each encoder output.
teaching_value: Shows students exactly how raw alignment scores are normalized into a probability distribution (attention weights) via softmax, clarifying why scores sum to 1 and how importance is distributed across inputs.
when_to_show: Show immediately after explaining alignment score computation, to demonstrate the normalization step that converts scores into interpretable attention weights.
suggested_caption: Step 3: Softmax converts alignment scores into attention weights. (Source)

description: Diagram showing Phase 4 of Bahdanau attention: computing the context vector by element-wise multiplication of attention weights with encoder outputs, illustrating how higher-weighted encoder outputs contribute more to the context vector.
teaching_value: Makes concrete how attention weights modulate encoder output contributions — students can see that a weight near 1 amplifies an encoder output while a weight near 0 suppresses it, directly linking the math to intuition.
when_to_show: Show after softmax step to demonstrate how attention weights are applied to encoder outputs to form the context vector used by the decoder.
suggested_caption: Step 4: Weighted encoder outputs combine into the context vector. (Source)

description: Diagram illustrating Phase 5 of Bahdanau attention: the context vector is concatenated with the decoder output, fed into the decoder RNN cell to produce a new hidden state, and then passed through a linear classifier to predict the next word.
teaching_value: Closes the loop of the attention mechanism by showing how the context vector feeds back into the decoder RNN and ultimately produces a word prediction, helping students understand the recurrent nature of the process.
when_to_show: Show as the final step of the Bahdanau attention walkthrough to complete the end-to-end picture of how one decoding time step produces an output word.
suggested_caption: Step 5: Context vector + decoder RNN cell produces next word prediction. (Source)

description: Diagram showing the full decoding output step and transitioning to Luong (multiplicative) attention, illustrating how simple matrix multiplications between encoder and decoder states produce attention scores more efficiently than Bahdanau's additive approach.
teaching_value: Helps students contrast Bahdanau (additive) and Luong (multiplicative) attention by showing the simpler matrix multiplication scoring mechanism, and introduces the concept of global vs. local attention variants.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from Bahdanau to Luong attention, to highlight the architectural simplification and efficiency gain of multiplicative scoring.
suggested_caption: Luong multiplicative attention: matrix multiplication replaces additive scoring. (Source)

description: Diagram of a global attention mechanism showing how the decoder hidden state ht is compared to all encoder hidden states hs to produce a variable-length alignment vector at, which is then used to compute the context vector ct for the current decoding time step.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how global attention aggregates all encoder hidden states into a single context vector, helping students understand the difference between local and global attention and why alignment vectors are variable-length.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the global attention variant of Bahdanau attention, specifically after explaining the encoder-decoder bottleneck problem and before discussing the score function used for alignment.
suggested_caption: Global attention: decoder hidden state ht compared to all encoder states hs. (Paperspace Blog)

description: A flow diagram (Fig. 11.3.1) showing how attention pooling computes a weighted average of values. It illustrates the pipeline: queries and keys feed into an attention scoring function 'a', the scores pass through softmax to produce attention weights, which then weight the values to produce the output.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of the full attention computation pipeline — from raw scores through softmax normalization to the final weighted value aggregation — making the abstract formula tangible.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on attention mechanisms, before introducing specific scoring functions, to establish the overall computational structure students will be filling in.
suggested_caption: Attention pooling: scoring function + softmax yields weighted value average. (D2L.ai)

description: A code output visualization (likely a heatmap or tensor printout) from running the DotProductAttention class on a toy example with minibatch size 2, 10 keys/values of dimension 4, and valid lengths 2 and 6. Shows the resulting 2×1×4 output tensor or attention weight distribution.
teaching_value: Lets students verify their understanding of scaled dot-product attention by seeing concrete numerical outputs, connecting the abstract formula to actual computed values and demonstrating the effect of masking via valid lengths.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the DotProductAttention formula and before students run the code themselves, to set expectations for what correct output looks like.
suggested_caption: DotProductAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)

description: A second code output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing attention weight heatmaps for the two minibatch examples with valid lengths 2 and 6, illustrating how masking zeros out weights beyond valid positions.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how valid-length masking affects the attention weight distribution — students can see that positions beyond the valid length receive zero (or near-zero) weight after softmax.
when_to_show: Show alongside the DotProductAttention code walkthrough to illustrate the masking effect; pair with the additive attention heatmap for comparison.
suggested_caption: Attention weight heatmap for DotProductAttention with valid-length masking. (D2L.ai)

description: A third output visualization from the DotProductAttention example, likely showing the final output values or an additional view of the attention weights, reinforcing the 2×1×4 output shape and the effect of the scaling factor 1/√d.
teaching_value: Provides an additional concrete view of scaled dot-product attention outputs, helping students understand how the scaling factor and masking interact to produce the final attended representation.
when_to_show: Use when students are confused about why scaling by 1/√d matters, showing how it affects the magnitude of scores before softmax.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention output demonstrating 1/√d scaling effect. (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely a final summary plot or tensor output confirming the 2×1×4 result, possibly showing the attended value vectors for both minibatch examples.
teaching_value: Completes the DotProductAttention demonstration by showing the final attended output, allowing students to trace the full computation from query-key dot products through to the weighted value sum.
when_to_show: Show as the concluding step of the DotProductAttention walkthrough before transitioning to additive attention, to close the loop on the full computation.
suggested_caption: Final attended output of DotProductAttention: 2×1×4 minibatch result. (D2L.ai)

description: A code output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example with queries of shape (2,1,20), keys (2,10,2), values (2,10,4), and valid lengths (2,6). Likely shows the output tensor or attention weights, demonstrating how the MLP-based scoring function handles different query and key dimensionalities.
teaching_value: Shows students that additive attention can handle queries and keys of different dimensions (20-dim queries vs 2-dim keys), contrasting with dot-product attention which requires equal dimensions.
when_to_show: Show when introducing additive attention to highlight its key advantage over dot-product attention: the ability to handle queries and keys of different sizes.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention output with 20-dim queries and 2-dim keys. (D2L.ai)

description: A second output visualization from the AdditiveAttention example, likely an attention weight heatmap showing the distribution over 10 keys for both minibatch examples, with masking applied at positions 2 and 6 respectively.
teaching_value: Allows direct visual comparison with the DotProductAttention heatmap, helping students see that both methods produce similar attention patterns despite using different scoring mechanisms.
when_to_show: Show side-by-side with the DotProductAttention heatmap to help students compare the two scoring functions and understand when each is preferred.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention weight heatmap with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)

description: A third output visualization from the AdditiveAttention example, likely showing intermediate computations or the final 2×1×4 output tensor, demonstrating the role of the learnable weight matrices W_q, W_k, and w_v in the scoring function.
teaching_value: Helps students understand how the learnable parameters in additive attention transform queries and keys into a common hidden space before computing compatibility scores.
when_to_show: Use when explaining the parameter matrices W_q and W_k to show how they project queries and keys to the same dimensionality h before scoring.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention intermediate output showing learned projection effect. (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth and final output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely the final attended output tensor of shape 2×1×4, completing the demonstration of the additive attention mechanism.
teaching_value: Closes the AdditiveAttention demonstration, allowing students to confirm that despite the different scoring mechanism, the output shape and structure match DotProductAttention, reinforcing that both are valid attention implementations.
when_to_show: Show as the final step of the AdditiveAttention walkthrough, then prompt students to compare outputs with DotProductAttention to consolidate understanding of both methods.
suggested_caption: Final AdditiveAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor matching DotProductAttention shape. (D2L.ai)

description: Diagram showing the context vector in a seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture, visualizing a size-4 hidden state vector passed from encoder to decoder in machine translation
teaching_value: Concretizes the abstract concept of a 'context vector' by showing it as a fixed-size numerical array, helping students understand the information bottleneck between encoder and decoder
when_to_show: When introducing seq2seq models and explaining how information is compressed into a fixed-size vector; use before discussing attention to motivate why the bottleneck is a problem
suggested_caption: Context vector (size 4) passed from encoder to decoder — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram illustrating word embeddings and RNN mechanics, showing how words are converted to vectors and how hidden states are passed between RNN time steps during encoding
teaching_value: Establishes the visual language for understanding RNN internals — showing input vectors, hidden states, and time-step transitions — which is foundational for understanding attention mechanisms later
when_to_show: When explaining how words are fed into an RNN encoder; show before the context vector diagram to build up from word → embedding → hidden state → context
suggested_caption: Word embeddings feed into RNN time steps, producing hidden states — Jay Alammar

description: High-level diagram contrasting attention-based seq2seq with classic seq2seq, showing the encoder passing all hidden states (not just the last) to the decoder
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the key architectural difference that attention introduces — passing all encoder hidden states — making it clear why attention solves the context bottleneck problem
when_to_show: When transitioning from classic seq2seq to attention models; show immediately after explaining the context vector bottleneck to illustrate the proposed solution
suggested_caption: Attention: encoder passes all hidden states to decoder — Jay Alammar

description: Visualization of attention weights across input sentence words at each decoding step, showing which French input words the model focuses on when generating each English output word
teaching_value: Demonstrates that attention learns non-trivial, non-monotonic word alignments between language pairs, helping students understand that the model learns meaningful cross-lingual correspondences rather than simple positional matching
when_to_show: After explaining the attention mechanism mathematically; use this to show a concrete real-world example of what attention weights look like and why they are interpretable
suggested_caption: Attention weights reveal learned word alignments between French and English — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram of a standard seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture showing how a variable-length source sequence is compressed into a fixed-dimensional context vector by the RNN encoder, which is then used by the RNN decoder to generate the target sequence token by token.
teaching_value: Illustrates the bottleneck problem of fixed-shape context variables in vanilla seq2seq models, motivating why attention mechanisms are needed for long sequences.
when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on attention mechanisms to establish the baseline seq2seq model and highlight its limitation of compressing all source information into a single fixed vector.
suggested_caption: Vanilla seq2seq: encoder compresses source into fixed context vector. (D2L.ai)

description: Detailed architectural diagram of the Bahdanau attention-based seq2seq model, showing how the decoder at each time step computes attention weights over all encoder hidden states to produce a dynamic context vector, which is then used alongside the decoder hidden state to generate the next token.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how Bahdanau attention replaces the fixed context vector with a dynamic, weighted combination of encoder states, helping students understand the alignment mechanism and how it overcomes the bottleneck.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the motivation for attention, when explaining how the decoder query attends to encoder keys/values at each decoding step.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau attention: decoder dynamically attends to relevant encoder states. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve (loss vs. epoch or iteration) for the Seq2SeqAttentionDecoder model during initial training, showing the model's learning progress over time.
teaching_value: Allows students to verify that the attention-augmented seq2seq model trains correctly and converges, providing empirical evidence that the implementation is functioning as expected.
when_to_show: Show when walking through the training procedure of the Bahdanau attention model to confirm the model is learning and to discuss convergence behavior.
suggested_caption: Training loss curve for Seq2SeqAttentionDecoder implementation. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve for the Bahdanau attention seq2seq model, likely showing a different training run or framework variant (e.g., MXNet, JAX, or TensorFlow) compared to image 3.
teaching_value: Demonstrates reproducibility of training results across different implementations or hyperparameter settings, reinforcing understanding of the training process.
when_to_show: Show alongside other training curves when comparing implementations across frameworks or when discussing the effect of hyperparameters on training.
suggested_caption: Training loss for Bahdanau attention decoder (alternate framework). (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve for the Bahdanau attention seq2seq model, representing another framework or configuration variant.
teaching_value: Provides additional evidence of consistent training behavior across implementations, helping students understand that the attention mechanism is framework-agnostic.
when_to_show: Show when comparing training results across multiple frameworks to reinforce that the Bahdanau attention mechanism behaves consistently.
suggested_caption: Training loss for Bahdanau attention model, third framework variant. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve for the Bahdanau attention seq2seq model, representing a fourth framework or configuration variant.
teaching_value: Completes the cross-framework comparison of training curves, showing consistent convergence behavior of the Bahdanau attention model.
when_to_show: Show as part of a complete set of training curves when discussing cross-framework reproducibility of the Bahdanau attention implementation.
suggested_caption: Training loss for Bahdanau attention model, fourth framework variant. (D2L.ai)

description: Attention weight heatmap visualizing which encoder hidden states (source tokens) the decoder attends to at each decoding time step when translating an English sentence to French, showing the alignment pattern learned by the Bahdanau attention mechanism.
teaching_value: Provides intuitive visual evidence of how the attention mechanism learns soft alignments between source and target tokens, making the abstract concept of dynamic context vectors concrete and interpretable.
when_to_show: Show after training is complete when explaining how attention weights can be interpreted as alignment scores, and when discussing the interpretability advantage of attention over fixed context vectors.
suggested_caption: Attention heatmap: learned alignment between English source and French target. (D2L.ai)

description: Attention weight heatmap for a second English-to-French translation example, showing the soft alignment pattern produced by the trained Bahdanau attention model.
teaching_value: Provides a second example of attention alignment, allowing students to compare patterns across different sentences and understand how attention generalizes across inputs.
when_to_show: Show alongside the first attention heatmap to compare alignment patterns across different translation examples and reinforce understanding of dynamic attention.
suggested_caption: Attention alignment heatmap for second English-French translation example. (D2L.ai)

description: Attention weight heatmap for a third English-to-French translation example, displaying the decoder's attention distribution over encoder states at each generation step.
teaching_value: Further illustrates the variability and specificity of attention alignments across different sentences, deepening understanding of how the model selectively focuses on relevant source tokens.
when_to_show: Show as part of a series of attention heatmaps when teaching students to interpret attention patterns and discuss cases where alignment is clear versus diffuse.
suggested_caption: Attention heatmap for third translation example showing selective focus. (D2L.ai)

description: Attention weight heatmap for a fourth English-to-French translation example, showing the final trained model's attention alignment pattern.
teaching_value: Completes the set of translation examples with attention visualizations, giving students a comprehensive view of how Bahdanau attention behaves across diverse inputs.
when_to_show: Show as the final example in a series of attention heatmaps to consolidate understanding of attention-based alignment before moving to Transformer architectures.
suggested_caption: Attention alignment heatmap for fourth English-French translation example. (D2L.ai)

description: Block diagram of multi-head attention: input queries (Q), keys (K), and values (V) are each linearly projected into h separate heads (different learned projection matrices per head), each head performs attention pooling in parallel, the h head outputs are concatenated, then passed through a final linear projection to produce the multi-head attention output.
teaching_value: Clarifies the computation pipeline and tensor flow in multi-head attention—how multiple learned subspace projections enable parallel attention behaviors and how their results are merged—helping students map the math (projections, attention, concat, output projection) to an implementable architecture.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks why multiple heads help, or when transitioning from single scaled dot-product attention to the multi-head formulation; also use it while tracing shapes/operations during implementation (project per head → attend → concat → final linear layer).
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: project Q/K/V into h heads, attend, concat, project (D2L).

description: A word-sequence attention illustration: one query vector is compared via dot products to each word’s key vector to produce attention weights (softmax implied), which are then used to compute a weighted average of the corresponding value vectors, yielding a context/output vector.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract Q–K–V mechanism concrete by showing the full pipeline (similarity scores → normalized weights → weighted sum), helping students connect the math definition of attention to an intuitive ‘focus over tokens’ interpretation.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the attention equation to map each symbol (Q,K,V, scores, weights) to a visual step; revisit when explaining why keys/values are per-token and how changing the query changes which tokens are emphasized.
suggested_caption: Attention over words: dot-product scores → weights → weighted value average (UvA DLC).

description: Block diagram of scaled dot-product attention: inputs Q, K, V; compute QKᵀ, scale by 1/√d_k, apply softmax to obtain attention matrix, then multiply by V to produce the attended outputs.
teaching_value: Clarifies the exact computation order and where scaling and softmax occur, which helps students implement attention correctly and understand why scaling stabilizes gradients for large d_k.
when_to_show: Use when transitioning from intuitive attention to the efficient batched/matrix form; also show during implementation walkthroughs to verify tensor shapes and operations.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention computation graph (UvA DLC).

description: Multi-head attention schematic: Q, K, V are linearly projected into multiple heads, each head runs scaled dot-product attention independently, head outputs are concatenated, then passed through a final linear projection.
teaching_value: Shows why multiple heads are more expressive than a single attention map by enabling parallel, different subspace projections and attention patterns, and connects the math (per-head projections + concat + output projection) to the architecture.
when_to_show: Show after students understand single-head attention; use it to explain what changes in code (reshape/split into heads, parallel attention, concat) and to motivate heads attending to different relations.
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads, concatenate, project (UvA DLC).

description: Transformer encoder block architecture: input embeddings plus positional encoding feed into multi-head self-attention, followed by residual connection and layer normalization, then a position-wise feed-forward network, again with residual connection and normalization; stacked across layers.
teaching_value: Provides the ‘big picture’ of how attention, residuals, normalization, and feed-forward sublayers compose into an encoder, helping students reason about data flow, where positional information enters, and why residual/normalization stabilize deep stacks.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer encoder or when debugging/reading implementations; use it to locate where to insert masks, dropout, and positional encodings in a real model.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + FFN with residuals and LayerNorm (UvA DLC).

description: Plot of sinusoidal positional encoding values across positions for selected hidden dimensions, illustrating sine on even indices and cosine on odd indices with different wavelengths per dimension.
teaching_value: Helps students see that positional encodings are structured periodic signals with dimension-dependent frequencies, supporting the intuition about representing relative shifts and enabling attention to use position information without recurrence.
when_to_show: Show right after presenting the PE(pos,i) formula to connect the equation to an interpretable signal; use it when explaining why adjacent dimensions form phase-shifted pairs.
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding across positions and dimensions (UvA DLC).

description: Heatmap visualization of positional encodings over sequence position (x-axis) and hidden dimension (y-axis), showing repeating wave patterns with increasing wavelengths for higher dimensions and phase shifts between neighboring dimensions.
teaching_value: Makes the full positional encoding matrix tangible, showing how different dimensions encode position at different scales; supports understanding of why Transformers can capture both short- and long-range positional patterns.
when_to_show: Use when students struggle to interpret PE beyond the formula; also useful when discussing how maximum sequence length and embedding size affect positional resolution.
suggested_caption: Heatmap of sinusoidal positional encodings (UvA DLC).

description: Learning-rate schedule plot for Transformer training with warm-up: learning rate increases linearly during early steps (warm-up) and then decays afterward according to a schedule (commonly inverse square-root).
teaching_value: Connects a training heuristic (warm-up) to its concrete schedule shape, helping students tune warm-up steps and understand stability issues early in training (e.g., Adam bias correction, LayerNorm-related gradient spikes).
when_to_show: Show when discussing training instabilities or reproducing Transformer baselines; use it to guide selection of warm-up steps and to explain why constant LR often fails early.
suggested_caption: Transformer learning-rate warm-up and decay schedule (UvA DLC).

description: Grid of attention maps for a sequence-to-sequence task: rows correspond to Transformer layers and columns to attention heads; each subplot is a SeqLen×SeqLen matrix showing where each token attends (row-wise softmax).
teaching_value: Demonstrates how attention patterns differ across heads and layers and provides evidence of learned algorithmic behavior (attending to a flipped index), helping students interpret attention tensors and diagnose whether a model learned the intended mapping.
when_to_show: Show after training when evaluating whether the model learned the task; use it to teach how to read attention matrices (query position as row, key position as column) and compare heads/layers.
suggested_caption: Attention maps by layer and head for a seq2seq task (UvA DLC).

description: Example set for anomaly detection built from CIFAR-100 images: a small collection of images where most belong to the same class and one image is from a different class (the anomaly).
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract ‘set anomaly detection’ problem in a concrete input/output example, clarifying what constitutes an anomaly and why order should not matter for the model.
when_to_show: Show before introducing the dataset sampling procedure and model; use it to confirm students understand the task definition (identify the odd-one-out) and the need for order-agnostic processing.
suggested_caption: CIFAR-100 set with one anomalous image among same-class images (UvA DLC).

description: Grid of multi-head self-attention heatmaps for a Transformer applied to set anomaly detection, showing attention probabilities between each pair of images in a 10-image set (9 similar tree images and 1 out-of-distribution volcano). Each subplot corresponds to a specific layer and head, with axes representing query image index attending to key image index; several heads concentrate attention on the last image, indicating it is treated as salient/anomalous.
teaching_value: Helps students interpret Transformer decisions by linking the anomaly prediction to internal attention patterns, illustrating how different heads specialize (e.g., some heads strongly attend to the outlier) and how attention maps can be used as a diagnostic tool for model behavior on sets.
when_to_show: After students understand self-attention computation, show this when explaining how to debug/interpret a set-based Transformer: ask them to identify which heads focus on the suspected outlier (last index) and relate that to the model’s anomaly prediction.
suggested_caption: Attention maps reveal heads focusing on the outlier image (UvA DLC).

description: Second grid of Transformer attention heatmaps across layers/heads for the same set anomaly detection example. It highlights contrasting behaviors: some mid-layer heads place high attention on the last (anomalous) image, while later-layer heads (Layer 4) assign it very low attention, and some heads (e.g., Layer 3 Head 2–4) distribute attention more evenly, resembling a weighted average over the set.
