teaching_value: Demonstrates that attention behavior can change across depth: earlier/mid layers may detect and route information about an outlier, while later layers may suppress it for aggregation; also illustrates the idea of 'average-of-set' heads used to form a reference representation for comparison in anomaly detection.
when_to_show: Use when discussing how different layers contribute differently to a decision: have learners compare Layer 2/3 vs Layer 4 patterns and hypothesize which heads compute a set summary versus which heads highlight anomalies.
suggested_caption: Layer-wise head behaviors: outlier-focused vs averaging attention (UvA DLC).

description: Full Transformer encoder–decoder architecture diagram showing stacked encoder and decoder blocks with multi-head self-attention, encoder–decoder attention, position-wise feed-forward networks, residual connections + layer normalization, and input/output embeddings with positional encodings leading to a linear + softmax output.
teaching_value: Helps students map the end-to-end dataflow of a Transformer and distinguish where self-attention vs cross-attention occurs, how stacking works, and how residual/normalization wrap each sublayer—key for implementing or debugging the model.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer at a high level, or when a learner confuses encoder self-attention with decoder masked self-attention and cross-attention; also useful before walking through tensor shapes per sublayer.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder–decoder with attention and feed-forward sublayers (Vaswani et al.).

description: Figure 2 composite: left panel illustrates Scaled Dot-Product Attention as Q·K^T scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k), masked/softmaxed to weights, then multiplied by V; right panel shows Multi-Head Attention splitting Q/K/V into multiple heads, applying attention in parallel, concatenating head outputs, and projecting with a final linear layer.
teaching_value: Makes the attention computation concrete as matrix operations and clarifies why scaling and softmax are used; also shows how multi-head attention increases representational capacity by attending to different subspaces in parallel.
when_to_show: Show when deriving the attention formula, explaining the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling, or when transitioning from single-head attention to multi-head attention and discussing parallel heads.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention and multi-head attention schematic (Vaswani et al.).

description: A larger rendering of Figure 2 emphasizing the multi-head attention pipeline: separate linear projections create per-head Q/K/V, each head computes scaled dot-product attention, head outputs are concatenated, then passed through an output projection.
teaching_value: Provides a clearer view of the multi-head decomposition and recombination steps, helping students understand implementation details like per-head dimensions, parallel attention blocks, and the final output projection.
when_to_show: Show when a learner needs more visual clarity on how heads are formed and recombined, especially during implementation discussions (e.g., reshaping tensors into [batch, heads, seq, d_k]).
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: project, attend per head, concatenate, project (Vaswani et al.).

description: Attention visualization for the encoder self-attention (layer 5 of 6) focusing on the token “making”; colored lines/weights from “making” to other tokens show multiple heads attending strongly to a distant phrase completion “making … more difficult.”
teaching_value: Demonstrates that self-attention can capture long-range dependencies without recurrence, making the abstract claim about modeling distant relationships tangible through head-specific attention patterns.
when_to_show: Show after explaining self-attention to illustrate how it links distant words; use when comparing Transformers to RNNs on handling long-range dependencies.
suggested_caption: Encoder self-attention links “making” to distant dependency (Vaswani et al.).

description: Anaphora-related attention visualization: top shows full attention patterns for a specific head (head 5) in encoder layer 5/6; bottom isolates attention originating from the word “its” for heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused attention to likely antecedents.
teaching_value: Helps students see that different heads can learn discourse-level functions like coreference/anaphora resolution, and that attention distributions can be sharp (nearly one-hot) for certain tokens.
when_to_show: Show when discussing interpretability of attention heads or giving examples of emergent linguistic structure in Transformers; useful in lessons on coreference signals.
suggested_caption: Attention heads focusing “its” on antecedents (Vaswani et al.).

description: Second panel associated with Figure 4: additional attention visualization emphasizing isolated attentions from the token “its” across two heads (5 and 6), highlighting concentrated links to specific earlier tokens.
teaching_value: Reinforces how head-to-head differences appear even for the same query token, supporting lessons on why multiple heads are useful and how attention can encode coreference cues.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks why multiple heads are needed or how to interpret per-head attention maps; pair with a discussion of limitations of attention-as-explanation.
suggested_caption: Isolated “its” attentions across heads show coreference-like links (Vaswani et al.).

description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 1 from encoder self-attention layer 5/6: a single head’s attention pattern aligns tokens in a way that appears to reflect syntactic structure (e.g., grouping related words/phrases).
teaching_value: Shows that attention heads can learn different structural roles, helping students connect attention patterns to syntax-like relationships and motivating analysis of head specialization.
when_to_show: Show when teaching that heads can learn different tasks (syntax vs coreference vs locality); use as a prompt for students to hypothesize what a head is tracking.
suggested_caption: Example head showing sentence-structure-related attention (Vaswani et al.).

description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 2 from a different encoder self-attention head (layer 5/6), contrasting with the previous example to show distinct learned attention behaviors across heads.
teaching_value: Provides a comparative example that different heads attend differently on the same sentence, supporting the idea that multi-head attention enables diverse relational features to be captured simultaneously.
when_to_show: Show immediately after image 7 to compare two heads; use when explaining that heads are not redundant and can encode different relational inductive biases.
suggested_caption: A second head with different structure-focused attention behavior (Vaswani et al.).

alt: Three diagrams showing dot product, general, and concat attention score computation flows.
description: Three side-by-side computation graphs comparing Luong attention score functions: (1) dot product scoring between decoder state h_t and encoder state h_s; (2) general/bilinear scoring using a learned matrix W_a between h_t and h_s; (3) concat scoring that concatenates h_t and h_s, applies a learned layer W_a with tanh, then projects with v_a^T to a scalar alignment score.
teaching_value: Helps learners map each named score function to its exact mathematical operations and parameters, making it easier to choose an attention mechanism based on expressiveness, dimensional constraints, and compute cost.
when_to_show: Show when a student asks how dot/general/concat differ, or before deriving attention weights (softmax over scores) so they can identify where parameters enter and why dot product requires matching hidden sizes.
suggested_caption: Luong score functions: dot, general (W_a), concat (tanh + v_a). (Brenndörfer)

alt: Log-scale line plot showing computational cost scaling with sequence length for three attention methods.
description: Log-scale line plot of FLOPs versus source sequence length S comparing dot product, general (bilinear), and concat attention scoring; dot product grows most slowly and remains lowest, general is higher due to an extra matrix multiply, and concat is highest due to additional layer and projection.
teaching_value: Makes the asymptotic and practical compute differences tangible, reinforcing why dot-product attention is favored on modern hardware and how extra parameters translate into higher per-step computation.
when_to_show: Show when discussing performance trade-offs or selecting an attention mechanism for long sequences; use it to justify choosing dot product when speed/throughput is a constraint.
suggested_caption: FLOPs vs sequence length for Luong score functions. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Bar chart comparing parameter counts across hidden dimensions for three attention methods.
description: Bar chart comparing parameter counts across hidden dimensions d for dot product (no parameters), general attention (one d×d matrix W_a), and concat attention (matrix W_a plus vector v_a), showing parameter growth with d and the relative overhead of each method.
teaching_value: Connects model capacity and memory footprint to the score-function choice, helping students anticipate overfitting risk and deployment constraints (e.g., parameter budget) as hidden size increases.
when_to_show: Show when a student is tuning hidden size or comparing architectures under a parameter budget; pair with the FLOPs plot to separate compute cost from parameter cost.
suggested_caption: Parameter counts for dot, general, and concat attention vs hidden size. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Heatmap showing attention weights across all encoder positions for global attention, with a diagonal bias.
description: Heatmap of global attention weights for 10 target time steps over a 12-token source sequence, with a strong diagonal band indicating roughly monotonic alignment while still assigning non-zero probability mass across most source positions.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates what 'global' means: every decoder step can attend to all encoder positions, enabling reordering and long-range dependencies while often learning near-diagonal alignments in translation-like tasks.
when_to_show: Show after introducing attention weights/softmax to illustrate typical alignment patterns; use it when contrasting global vs local attention and discussing O(S) per-step cost.
suggested_caption: Global attention heatmap: broad support with diagonal alignment. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Heatmap showing attention weights concentrated in a narrow diagonal band for local attention.
description: Heatmap of local attention weights over the same target/source lengths, where non-zero weights appear only in a narrow diagonal window around a predicted alignment point p_t, corresponding to a window half-width D=2.
teaching_value: Clarifies how local attention reduces computation by restricting scoring to a small neighborhood, making the O(D) per-step idea concrete and showing the trade-off between efficiency and flexibility.
when_to_show: Show when a student asks how local attention works or why it is faster; use it to explain monotonic/local-m assumptions and the effect of changing D on coverage.
suggested_caption: Local attention heatmap with window D=2 around alignment point. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Flow diagram showing Bahdanau attention with context computed before the RNN step using the previous hidden state.
description: Flow diagram of Bahdanau attention placement: context vector is computed using the previous decoder hidden state h_{t-1} before the RNN update, then the context is concatenated with the current input and fed into the RNN to produce h_t.
teaching_value: Helps students understand architectural placement: attention as input changes the RNN state update by injecting source context directly, which affects information flow and can change training dynamics compared to Luong.
when_to_show: Show when comparing Bahdanau vs Luong or when a student is confused about whether attention uses h_{t-1} or h_t; use it to trace the computation order step-by-step.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau: compute context from h_{t-1} before RNN update. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Flow diagram showing Luong attention with context computed after the RNN step using the current hidden state.
description: Flow diagram of Luong attention placement: the decoder RNN runs first to produce the current hidden state h_t, then attention scores and the context vector are computed from h_t and encoder states, and finally a learned combination produces the attentional state \tilde{h}_t for output prediction.
teaching_value: Makes the 'attention as output' pipeline explicit, showing why Luong uses the current state h_t in scoring and how the attentional state \tilde{h}_t is formed for the final softmax prediction.
when_to_show: Show when implementing Luong attention or debugging tensor flow; use it to explain the order: RNN → scores/weights → context → combine → logits.
suggested_caption: Luong: compute attention after RNN, combine into \tilde{h}_t for output. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Bar chart showing dot product attention weights across source positions.
description: Bar chart of dot-product attention weights over 10 source positions for one example, showing a peaked distribution with near-zero weights on some positions (including padded/masked positions).
teaching_value: Gives an intuitive feel for how dot-product similarity translates into a probability distribution over source tokens, and provides a quick sanity check for masking and normalization (weights summing to 1).
when_to_show: Show when validating an implementation (check masking and sum-to-one) or when comparing how different score functions change distribution sharpness after training.
suggested_caption: Dot-product attention: peaked weights across source positions. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Bar chart showing general attention weights across source positions.
description: Bar chart of general (bilinear) attention weights over the same 10 source positions, illustrating a distribution shaped by the learned matrix W_a and differing from the dot-product pattern.
teaching_value: Helps learners see the practical effect of adding W_a: the model can reweight feature interactions between encoder and decoder states, producing different alignments even on the same inputs.
when_to_show: Show right after the dot-product bar chart to contrast parameter-free vs learned scoring; use it when explaining why general attention can work with differing representation spaces.
suggested_caption: General attention: W_a reshapes alignment weights vs dot product. (Brenndörfer)

alt: Bar chart showing concat attention weights across source positions.
description: Bar chart of concat (additive) attention weights over source token positions for one decoder step, with two rightmost positions shaded gray to indicate padding; non-padded weights sum to ~1 and padded positions receive near-zero weight due to masking.
teaching_value: Helps students verify two core properties of attention implementations: (1) softmax-normalized weights form a probability distribution over real tokens, and (2) padding masks correctly suppress attention to padded timesteps.
when_to_show: Show when debugging or explaining why attention must ignore padding; ask the learner to check that padded bars are ~0 and remaining bars sum to 1.
suggested_caption: Concat attention weights with padding masked (Marc Brenndörfer).

alt: Heatmap showing attention weights with a diagonal pattern indicating word alignment between English source and French target sentences.
description: Attention heatmap for a simulated English-to-French translation: rows correspond to generated French target words and columns to English source words; strongest weights form an approximately diagonal band with a notable off-diagonal shift where French adjective-noun order differs (e.g., 'noir' attending to 'black').
teaching_value: Makes the abstract idea of alignment concrete by showing how attention tracks word-to-word correspondences across time, and how reordering appears as systematic deviations from a diagonal alignment.
when_to_show: Use after introducing attention weights to illustrate alignment patterns; prompt learners to identify diagonal vs. reordered alignments and relate them to source/target word order.
suggested_caption: Simulated translation alignment heatmap (Marc Brenndörfer).

alt: Bar chart showing attention weights with 'cat' receiving the highest weight around 0.7 highlighted in red.
description: Bar chart of attention weights over the source words 'The', 'cat', 'sat' when generating the French word 'chat'; the 'cat' bar is highlighted in red at roughly 0.7, with the remaining probability mass split between the other two words.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how softmax turns alignment scores into a peaked distribution and how semantic correspondence emerges as a dominant weight on the relevant source token.
concepts: encoder hidden states vs decoder hidden state similarity
when_to_show: Show when walking through a single decoding step; ask the learner to interpret the distribution and explain why 'cat' should dominate when producing 'chat'.
suggested_caption: Attention focuses on 'cat' when generating 'chat' (Marc Brenndörfer).

alt: Stacked bar chart showing weighted contributions from each encoder state to context vector dimensions.
description: Stacked bar chart across context-vector dimensions showing each dimension as a weighted sum of encoder-state components; contributions are color-coded by source word with 'cat' (red) dominating, and diamond markers indicating the final context vector values matching the stacked totals.
teaching_value: Connects the probability view of attention to the linear-algebra computation of the context vector, helping students see that attention is a convex combination applied independently per feature dimension.
concepts: weighted sum (convex combination) of encoder states
when_to_show: Use immediately after presenting attention weights to show how they produce the context vector; have learners compute one dimension manually from the displayed contributions.
suggested_caption: Context vector as weighted encoder-state sum (Marc Brenndörfer).

alt: Grouped bar chart comparing encoder state values with the resulting context vector.
description: Grouped bar chart comparing each encoder state’s feature values (including the 'cat' state in red) against the resulting context vector; the context vector closely matches the 'cat' encoding but is slightly shifted toward 'The' and 'sat' due to their smaller weights.
teaching_value: Helps students build intuition that the context vector interpolates between source representations—often resembling the highest-attended token while still incorporating small contributions from others.
when_to_show: Show after computing the context vector to interpret its meaning; ask learners which encoder state it most resembles and why it is not identical.
suggested_caption: Context vector resembles 'cat' but blends all tokens (Marc Brenndörfer).

alt: Line plot showing score standard deviation growing with hidden dimension for unscaled versus scaled attention.
description: Line plot of the standard deviation of dot-product attention scores versus hidden dimension d for random unit-variance vectors: unscaled dot products (red) grow roughly with sqrt(d), while scaled dot products dividing by sqrt(d) (green) keep variance near 1 across dimensions.
teaching_value: Explains why scaling by 1/sqrt(d) stabilizes score magnitudes as dimensionality grows, preventing overly large logits that would destabilize softmax and gradients.
when_to_show: Use when motivating scaled dot-product attention (e.g., before transformers); ask learners to predict what happens to score variance as d increases with and without scaling.
suggested_caption: Scaling keeps dot-product score variance stable (Marc Brenndörfer).

alt: Grouped bar chart showing attention weight distributions becoming sharper as score magnitude increases.
description: Grouped bar charts showing softmax attention weights for increasing score scales (e.g., 1 to 50): as scale increases, probability mass concentrates almost entirely on the highest-scoring position, illustrating increasingly sharp/peaked attention.
teaching_value: Shows how large-magnitude logits cause softmax saturation, leading to near one-hot attention and reduced gradient signal—linking dot-product magnitude to training difficulties.
when_to_show: Show when discussing why unscaled dot-product attention can be problematic at high dimensions; have learners relate sharper distributions to poorer gradient flow.
suggested_caption: Larger score scales make softmax attention nearly one-hot (Marc Brenndörfer).

description: High-level schematic of a Multi-Head Attention block showing inputs (queries, keys, values), multiple parallel attention heads, concatenation of head outputs, and a final linear projection to produce the layer output.
teaching_value: Gives learners the mental model of MHA as 'parallel attention + concat + projection', which helps them map later tensor reshapes and projections to concrete sub-steps in the architecture.
when_to_show: Show before any math/implementation to orient students, then revisit when explaining why tensors are reshaped into (heads, seq_len, d_head).
suggested_caption: Multi-Head Attention overview (after Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Scaled dot-product attention diagram: Q and K are multiplied (QK^T), scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k), passed through softmax to form attention weights, then multiplied by V to yield the attention output.
teaching_value: Connects the formula Attention(Q,K,V)=softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V to the computational pipeline, clarifying where scaling and softmax occur and what the attention weights represent.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the attention equation or debugging shape errors around QK^T and softmax axes.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention computation (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Canonical Multi-Head Attention diagram from the Transformer paper: inputs are linearly projected into multiple Q/K/V sets, attention is computed per head in parallel, head outputs are concatenated, then projected to the final output.
teaching_value: Helps students understand why multiple smaller attention computations can outperform one large one, and provides a reference for implementing efficient projections and concatenation across heads.
when_to_show: Show right before deriving the MHA formula and again when explaining efficient implementation (single QKV projection then reshape/split).
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention block (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Line chart comparing training and test loss curves for two models/implementations, showing similar loss trajectories over epochs.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that an optimized/efficient MHA implementation can match the learning behavior of a baseline implementation, reinforcing correctness beyond unit tests.
when_to_show: Show after presenting an optimized projection/reshape approach to justify that speedups did not change model behavior.
suggested_caption: Train vs test loss for two MHA implementations (Subedi).

description: Plot showing multiple loss curves (e.g., across model variants) used to compare training outcomes when adding positional embeddings versus other setups.
teaching_value: Supports comparative reasoning: learners can see how positional embeddings affect convergence and generalization relative to other configurations.
when_to_show: Show during an ablation discussion: after training multiple variants and before interpreting accuracy/probability differences on reordered sentences.
suggested_caption: Loss comparison across variants including positional embeddings (Subedi).

description: Attention weight heatmaps for the sentence "can you can that", comparing models without positional embeddings (identical patterns for repeated 'can') versus with positional embeddings (different attention patterns by position).
teaching_value: Visually proves why positional information matters: identical tokens at different positions become distinguishable, changing attention distributions and downstream predictions.
when_to_show: Show when explaining failure cases without positional embeddings (word order invariance) and how positional embeddings break that symmetry.
suggested_caption: Attention weights with/without positional embeddings on "can you can that" (Subedi).

description: A schematic showing an input token sequence represented as a 2D array X with shape (N, D): N tokens (rows) each with D-dimensional embeddings (columns).
teaching_value: Clarifies the baseline tensor shape used throughout self-attention, helping students track how projections (Q, K, V) and attention outputs preserve the token axis while transforming feature dimensions.
when_to_show: Show before introducing Q/K/V projections or any attention math, when a learner is confused about what N and D represent or how shapes propagate.
suggested_caption: Input embeddings as an (N, D) matrix (Eli Bendersky).

description: A schematic of a batched input tensor X with shape (B, N, D), adding a batch dimension B in front of the sequence length N and embedding size D.
teaching_value: Helps students generalize single-sequence attention to practical training/inference where operations are vectorized over batches, reinforcing that attention is applied independently per batch element.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from single-example attention to batched implementations, or when debugging shape mismatches involving batch dimensions.
suggested_caption: Batched embeddings tensor shaped (B, N, D) (Eli Bendersky).

alt: attention paper screenshot showing learned attention
description: A screenshot from the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper illustrating learned attention patterns as an attention matrix/heatmap, where rows attend to columns with varying strengths.
teaching_value: Connects the abstract (N, N) attention matrix to an interpretable visualization, showing that each row forms a probability distribution over tokens and that high weights indicate strong token-to-token relationships.
when_to_show: Show after defining the attention matrix (softmax(QK^T/√d)) to build intuition about what the weights mean and how to read rows/columns.
suggested_caption: Example learned attention weights from AIAYN (Vaswani et al.).

description: A multi-head attention diagram showing multiple parallel attention heads, each with its own Q/K/V projections producing (N, HS) outputs, concatenated across heads and passed through a final linear projection back to (N, D).
teaching_value: Makes the parallelism and dimensional bookkeeping of multi-head attention concrete: separate subspaces per head, concatenation, and the role of the output projection in restoring model dimension.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks why multiple heads exist or how NH and HS relate to D, especially before implementing concatenation and the final projection.
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads, concat, then project (Eli Bendersky).

description: A causal masking illustration of an (N, N) attention matrix where upper-triangular (future) positions are greyed out/blocked so each token can only attend to itself and earlier tokens; remaining (blue) entries per row sum to 1 after softmax.
teaching_value: Visually explains why and how causal masks prevent information leakage in autoregressive decoding, linking the mask to the constraint 'no attending to future tokens' and to row-wise softmax normalization.
when_to_show: Show when introducing decoder blocks or when explaining why training without masking lets the model 'cheat' by using future tokens.
suggested_caption: Causal mask blocks attention to future tokens (Eli Bendersky).

description: A block-matrix diagram of basic matrix multiplication, highlighting how multiplying a left matrix by a right matrix produces an output via row-by-column dot products, with colored blocks indicating submatrices.
teaching_value: Builds intuition for later vectorization tricks by grounding them in matmul semantics, helping students see how partitioning matrices affects corresponding partitions in the result.
when_to_show: Show right before explaining head-vectorization via stacking/concatenating weights, when a learner needs a refresher on why block structure in RHS maps to block structure in the output.
suggested_caption: Matmul viewed in blocks to motivate vectorization (Eli Bendersky).

description: A block-matrix diagram showing concatenation of two RHS matrices side-by-side (along columns) so a single matmul computes two outputs at once; colored regions indicate that shared RHS blocks yield identical corresponding output blocks.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the key optimization idea: combine multiple linear projections into one larger matmul, which directly motivates vectorizing multi-head attention by stacking per-head weight matrices.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to replace per-head loops with a single batched/stacked matmul, especially for GPU/TPU-friendly implementations.
suggested_caption: Concatenate RHS blocks to compute multiple matmuls at once (Eli Bendersky).

description: Scatter/summary plot comparing observed block forward mismatch tail (95th percentile of r_block = ||E||/||X||) against a combined first-order predictor, aggregated per configuration (precision FP32/BF16/FP16, width d_model in {96,128}, many seeds). Points are color-coded by precision and emphasize tail behavior rather than mean error.
teaching_value: Helps students see how a theoretically motivated predictor tracks worst-case (tail) numerical error across model configurations, and how precision changes shift mismatch levels—supporting the idea that instability risk can be predicted from logged conditioning/proxy metrics.
when_to_show: Show after defining r_block and the predictor terms, to motivate why tail summaries are used and to compare risk across precisions and widths.
suggested_caption: Tail forward mismatch vs combined predictor across precisions (arXiv).

description: Raw scatter plot of observed forward mismatch tail versus predictor values for individual runs (each point corresponds to a specific seed/width/precision setting), showing the unaggregated spread and clustering by precision.
teaching_value: Lets learners distinguish per-run variability from aggregated trends, illustrating how seeds and width contribute noise and how precision groups separate before any scaling or normalization.
when_to_show: Use when a student asks whether the summary trend hides outliers or seed effects; pair with the aggregated plot to discuss variance vs signal.
suggested_caption: Per-run mismatch vs predictor scatter by seed/width/precision (arXiv).

description: Scatter plot like Image 2 but with predictors scaled by machine epsilon (ε_mach), showing BF16 and FP16 points collapsing onto a shared linear relationship with observed forward mismatch, indicating cross-precision alignment after scaling.
teaching_value: Demonstrates a key numerical-analysis idea: rounding-error-driven effects scale with ε_mach, so rescaling predictors can unify behavior across precisions and improve linear predictability.
when_to_show: Show when introducing why ε_mach matters in floating-point error models, or when comparing FP16 vs BF16 stability using a single calibrated predictor.
suggested_caption: Scaling by ε_mach collapses BF16/FP16 mismatch–predictor relation (arXiv).

description: Two time-series (example run, seed 0) showing κ_softmax and forward mismatch r_block over training steps, with κ_softmax peaks occurring earlier; the plot highlights an approximate 16-step lead of κ_softmax relative to mismatch spikes.
teaching_value: Supports forecasting: students can see how a conditioning/stability proxy (κ_softmax) can act as an early warning signal for upcoming numerical mismatch spikes, motivating lead–lag analysis and spike prediction metrics.
when_to_show: Use when teaching how to validate an early-warning metric: point out the lag, then discuss how to choose horizons and evaluate Precision@K for spike prediction.
suggested_caption: κ_softmax leads forward mismatch spikes by ~16 steps (arXiv).

description: Summary chart of Exp-3 showing changes in tail loss across settings of (ρ*, cap) for an LN-ε adjustment rule; values are plotted so positive indicates improved (reduced) tail loss relative to baseline.
teaching_value: Helps learners evaluate a mitigation strategy by scanning hyperparameter trade-offs and seeing whether stabilizing interventions improve worst-case loss behavior, not just average performance.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the LN-ε update rule and ρ_LN(ε)<1 condition, to discuss which parameter regions improve tail loss and how to pick defaults.
suggested_caption: Tail loss change under LN-ε mitigation across (ρ*, cap) (arXiv).

description: Summary chart of Exp-3 showing change in tail forward mismatch (r_block tail) under the same LN-ε mitigation sweep; the plot indicates the forward-error tail changes are small across (ρ*, cap).
teaching_value: Teaches that improving tail loss does not necessarily reduce measured forward mismatch tails, prompting discussion about which metrics an intervention targets and how numerical proxies relate to downstream loss.
when_to_show: Use when comparing outcome metrics: after showing tail loss improvements, surface this to emphasize that mitigation effects can be metric-specific and to guide multi-metric evaluation.
suggested_caption: LN-ε mitigation yields small changes in tail forward mismatch (arXiv).

description: Mathematical interval notation “(0,1)”, indicating values strictly between 0 and 1.
teaching_value: Clarifies the range constraint for softmax outputs: each component is a valid probability (non-inclusive bounds).
when_to_show: When explaining why softmax outputs can be interpreted as probabilities and must be strictly between 0 and 1.
suggested_caption: Softmax outputs lie in the open interval (0,1). (Wikipedia)

description: Function signature “σ: ℝ^K → (0,1)^K”, mapping a real K-dimensional vector to a K-dimensional vector with entries in (0,1).
teaching_value: Shows the domain and codomain of softmax, emphasizing it transforms arbitrary real scores into probability-like values per class.
when_to_show: When introducing softmax formally, before presenting the component-wise formula.
suggested_caption: Softmax maps ℝ^K to (0,1)^K. (Wikipedia)

description: Constraint “K > 1”, stating softmax is defined for more than one class/component.
teaching_value: Prevents confusion with the binary case (logistic/sigmoid) and frames softmax as a multi-class generalization.
when_to_show: When contrasting sigmoid (K=2) with softmax (K>1) or defining the multiclass setting.
suggested_caption: Softmax is used for K>1 classes. (Wikipedia)

description: Input vector definition “z = (z1, …, zK) ∈ ℝ^K”, naming the K real-valued logits/scores.
teaching_value: Establishes notation for the inputs (logits) that softmax normalizes, helping students parse the later formula.
when_to_show: Right before deriving or applying the softmax formula to a concrete vector of scores.
suggested_caption: Input logits vector z in ℝ^K. (Wikipedia)

description: Output membership statement “σ(z) ∈ (0,1)^K”, indicating each output component is strictly between 0 and 1.
teaching_value: Reinforces that softmax produces probability-like components, preparing for the additional constraint that they sum to 1.
when_to_show: When summarizing properties of softmax outputs after introducing the mapping and before discussing normalization (sum-to-one).
suggested_caption: Softmax output vector has entries in (0,1). (Wikipedia)

description: Component-wise softmax equation: σ(z)_i = e^{z_i} / (∑_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}).
teaching_value: Provides the core computation showing exponentiation and normalization, explaining why outputs are positive and sum to 1.
when_to_show: When teaching how to compute softmax probabilities from logits, including step-by-step numeric examples.
suggested_caption: Softmax component formula normalizes exponentiated logits. (Wikipedia)

description: Symbol “z_i”, denoting the i-th component of the input vector z (a single logit/score).
teaching_value: Helps students interpret indices in the softmax equation and connect scalar components to the full vector.
when_to_show: When a learner is confused by subscripts in σ(z)_i or when unpacking the formula into per-class computations.
suggested_caption: z_i denotes the i-th input logit. (Wikipedia)

description: Bold symbol “z”, representing the input vector to softmax.
teaching_value: Distinguishes vector input from scalar components, aiding correct reading of σ(z) versus z_i.
when_to_show: When introducing notation conventions (bold vectors) or clarifying the difference between z and z_i.
suggested_caption: z denotes the input vector of logits. (Wikipedia)

description: Symbol “K”, representing the number of components/classes in the softmax input/output.
teaching_value: Anchors the summation limits and dimensionality, making clear softmax scales to any number of classes.
when_to_show: When explaining the denominator sum ∑_{j=1}^{K} and how softmax generalizes to K classes.
suggested_caption: K is the number of classes/components. (Wikipedia)

description: Expression “σ(z)”, denoting the full softmax output vector (probability distribution over K outcomes).
teaching_value: Connects the component formula to the vector-valued output used in multiclass prediction and neural network outputs.
when_to_show: When moving from per-component computation to interpreting the entire output as a distribution over classes.
suggested_caption: σ(z) is the softmax probability vector. (Wikipedia)

description: A math-rendered input vector example: (1, 2, 8), used as a softmax input tuple z.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete numeric input to demonstrate how exponentiation and normalization make the largest logit dominate the probability distribution.
when_to_show: When introducing softmax, right before computing softmax numerically on a simple 3-class example.
suggested_caption: Example logits vector (1,2,8) used for softmax (Wikipedia).

description: A math-rendered output probability vector example: (0.001, 0.002, 0.997), presented as the approximate softmax of (1, 2, 8).
teaching_value: Shows the key effect of softmax: converting logits into a probability distribution that heavily favors the maximum input component.
when_to_show: After walking through the softmax formula to confirm the computed probabilities and interpret them as near one-hot.

description: An equation snippet defining a change of base parameterization: b = e^β.
teaching_value: Connects using an arbitrary exponential base b to the common temperature/inverse-temperature scaling form via β, clarifying how scaling affects sharpness.
when_to_show: When explaining how softmax can be generalized with a base b or with a scaling factor β (temperature).
suggested_caption: Parameterizing base b as e^β for scaled softmax (Wikipedia).

description: An equation snippet defining an alternative parameterization: b = e^{-β}.
teaching_value: Highlights that flipping the sign of β reverses preference (favoring minima instead of maxima), useful for discussing softmin vs softmax.
when_to_show: When contrasting softmax (favoring large logits) with softmin (favoring small logits) via negative scaling.
suggested_caption: Alternative base parameterization b = e^{-β} (Wikipedia).

alt: {\displaystyle \sigma (\mathbf {z} )_{i}={\frac {e^{\beta z_{i}}}{\sum _{j=1}^{K}e^{\beta z_{j}}}}{\text{ or }}\sigma (\mathbf {z} )_{i}={\frac {e^{-\beta z_{i}}}{\sum _{j=1}^{K}e^{-\beta z_{j}}}}{\text{ for }}i=1,\dotsc ,K.}
description: Two equivalent scaled softmax formulas: σ(z)_i = e^{β z_i}/∑_j e^{β z_j} or σ(z)_i = e^{-β z_i}/∑_j e^{-β z_j}, for i=1,…,K.
teaching_value: Makes explicit how a scaling factor β controls distribution sharpness and how negating β yields a softmin-like behavior.
when_to_show: When a student asks how temperature changes confidence/peakedness or how to implement softmax with a tunable sharpness.
suggested_caption: Scaled softmax/softmin forms with parameter β (Wikipedia).

description: A physics-style definition of inverse temperature: β = 1/(kT).
teaching_value: Links machine-learning temperature scaling to statistical mechanics, helping students interpret β as inverse temperature controlling randomness/entropy.
when_to_show: When motivating temperature scaling intuitively (high T → flatter; low T → peakier) or connecting softmax to Boltzmann distributions.
suggested_caption: Inverse temperature relation β = 1/(kT) (Wikipedia).

description: A math snippet indicating the index range for components: 1, …, n.
teaching_value: Clarifies that argmax/softargmax outputs are defined component-wise over n positions, preparing students to read the one-hot vector definition.
when_to_show: Right before defining argmax as a one-hot vector over n entries, to orient students to indexing.
suggested_caption: Component indices run from 1 to n (Wikipedia).

description: A formal definition of argmax returning a one-hot vector: arg max(z1,…,zn) = (y1,…,yn) = (0,…,0,1,0,…,0).
teaching_value: Shows the target behavior softmax approximates: a discrete selection of the maximum as a one-hot indicator vector.
when_to_show: When explaining softmax as a smooth approximation to argmax (softargmax), contrasting discrete vs differentiable outputs.
suggested_caption: Argmax as a one-hot vector over n classes (Wikipedia).

description: A condition snippet from the argmax definition: y_i = 1 (for the maximizing index).
teaching_value: Emphasizes the indicator nature of argmax outputs, making it easier to compare with softmax probabilities approaching 1 for the max.
when_to_show: When unpacking the argmax definition into cases (y_i=1 for max index, 0 otherwise) before introducing softargmax.
suggested_caption: Argmax sets y_i = 1 at the maximizing index (Wikipedia).

description: A math snippet showing the index variable i, used in component-wise definitions of argmax/softmax outputs.
teaching_value: Helps students track which component is being discussed in piecewise definitions and summations, reducing notation confusion.
when_to_show: When a learner is confused by subscripts in σ(z)_i or y_i and needs a reminder that i denotes the component index.
suggested_caption: Index i denotes a specific vector component (Wikipedia).

description: Mathematical notation for an n-dimensional input vector: (z1, …, zn).
teaching_value: Clarifies that softmax/argmax operate on a vector of scores (logits), not a single scalar.
when_to_show: When introducing softmax as a function mapping a score vector to probabilities.
suggested_caption: Input score vector (z1,…,zn). (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Alternate typeset form of the n-dimensional input vector: (z1, …, zn).
teaching_value: Reinforces the vector-input convention used in defining argmax and softmax over n classes.
when_to_show: When a learner is confused about what z represents in softmax/argmax formulas.
suggested_caption: Vector of class scores (z1,…,zn). (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Example of one-hot argmax output: argmax(1,5,10) = (0,0,1).
teaching_value: Shows argmax as a one-hot vector, making it comparable to softmax outputs on the simplex.
when_to_show: When contrasting hard decisions (argmax) with soft decisions (softmax probabilities).
suggested_caption: One-hot argmax example. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Tie case for argmax represented by splitting mass: argmax(1,5,5) = (0, 1/2, 1/2).
teaching_value: Demonstrates how argmax can be generalized under ties to a probability-like vector.
when_to_show: When explaining what happens if multiple classes share the maximum score.
suggested_caption: Argmax tie splits mass equally. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: All-equal inputs yield uniform output: argmax(z,…,z) = (1/n,…,1/n).
teaching_value: Connects symmetry/equal scores to uniform distributions, a key intuition for softmax behavior.
when_to_show: When motivating why equal logits should produce equal probabilities under softmax.
suggested_caption: Equal scores imply uniform output. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Limit notation indicating the inverse-temperature parameter grows: β → ∞.
teaching_value: Signals the regime where softmax becomes increasingly peaked, approaching a hard argmax.
when_to_show: When introducing temperature scaling and the hardening limit of softmax.
suggested_caption: Hardening limit: β→∞. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Convergence statement: σβ(z) → argmax(z) as β increases.
teaching_value: Summarizes the core idea of softargmax: softmax approaches one-hot argmax in the limit.
when_to_show: When a student asks why softmax is called a smooth approximation to argmax.
suggested_caption: Softmax approaches argmax as β grows. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Near-tie example where second entry is slightly larger: σβ(1, 1.0001) → (0,1).
teaching_value: Illustrates sensitivity: an arbitrarily small advantage can dominate as β→∞.
when_to_show: When discussing numerical intuition for how small logit differences affect predicted class.
suggested_caption: Slightly larger logit wins as β→∞. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Near-tie example where first entry is slightly larger: σβ(1, 0.9999) → (1,0).
teaching_value: Complements the previous case to show the argmax flips when the ordering flips.
when_to_show: When emphasizing that only relative ordering matters in the β→∞ limit.
suggested_caption: Ordering determines the limiting one-hot output. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: Exact tie in 2D yields equal probabilities: σβ(1,1) = 1/2 (per component).
teaching_value: Shows that with equal logits, softmax outputs equal mass regardless of β, matching symmetry.
when_to_show: When explaining what softmax returns when two classes have identical scores.
suggested_caption: Equal logits give 1/2–1/2 split. (Wikipedia: Softmax function)

description: A plotted curve labeled (x, x), indicating the identity line where output equals input.
teaching_value: Helps contrast a smooth, continuous mapping with the discontinuous one-hot argmax, motivating why softargmax is introduced as a smooth approximation.
when_to_show: When introducing why argmax is not differentiable/continuous and you need a smooth surrogate for optimization.
suggested_caption: Identity line (x,x) as a smooth baseline (Wikipedia).

description: Limit notation showing beta approaching negative infinity (β → −∞).
teaching_value: Signals the low-temperature/high-negative-coldness regime where softargmax behavior flips toward selecting minima (argmin), clarifying how the parameter β controls the limiting discrete choice.
when_to_show: When explaining how changing the sign/magnitude of β changes softmax/softargmax from max-seeking to min-seeking.
suggested_caption: Limit β→−∞ corresponds to argmin behavior (Wikipedia).

description: Equation defining temperature as the reciprocal of beta: T = 1/β.
teaching_value: Connects machine-learning notation (β scaling logits) to statistical mechanics (temperature), enabling students to interpret ‘sharper’ vs ‘softer’ distributions via T.
when_to_show: When translating between β-parameterized softmax and temperature-parameterized softmax in explanations or derivations.
suggested_caption: Temperature–coldness relation: T=1/β (Wikipedia).

description: Worked numeric softmax example for inputs (0,10) at β=1: σ(0,10) ≈ (0.00005, 0.99995).
teaching_value: Demonstrates how a large logit gap yields an output nearly one-hot, illustrating pointwise convergence toward argmax and practical ‘almost argmax’ behavior.
when_to_show: When students ask how big a score gap is needed for softmax to behave like argmax at a given temperature.
suggested_caption: Softmax at β=1 for (0,10) is nearly one-hot (Wikipedia).

description: Worked numeric softmax example for inputs (0,10) at β=1/100: σ_{1/100}(0,10) ≈ (0.475, 0.525).
teaching_value: Shows that high temperature (small β) makes probabilities close to uniform even with a sizable score gap, reinforcing non-uniform convergence rates and temperature effects.
when_to_show: When comparing softmax outputs under different temperatures or explaining calibration/entropy control via β.
suggested_caption: High temperature softmax flattens probabilities (Wikipedia).

description: Limit notation indicating temperature goes to zero: T = 1/β → 0.
teaching_value: Encodes the ‘zero-temperature’ limit where softargmax/softmax becomes a hard argmax selection, tying limiting behavior to temperature rather than β.
when_to_show: When summarizing limiting cases: T→0 gives hard max; T→∞ gives uniform distribution.
suggested_caption: Zero-temperature limit T→0 yields hard selection (Wikipedia).

description: Index set notation {1, …, k}, referring to the set of possible states/classes.
teaching_value: Clarifies that softmax outputs form a distribution over a finite set of discrete outcomes (microstates/classes), grounding later probability expressions.
when_to_show: When mapping softmax components to class labels or microstates before writing probabilities P(y=j|x).
suggested_caption: Softmax outputs over indices {1,…,k} (Wikipedia).

description: Multinomial logistic regression probability: P(y=j|x)=exp(x^T w_j)/Σ_{k=1}^K exp(x^T w_k).
teaching_value: Provides the canonical application of softmax turning linear scores into normalized class probabilities, linking model scores to probabilistic interpretation.
when_to_show: When deriving or implementing multiclass logistic regression or explaining the final layer of a classifier.
suggested_caption: Softmax converts linear scores into class probabilities (Wikipedia).

description: Mapping showing K linear functions: x ↦ x^T w_1, …, x ↦ x^T w_K.
teaching_value: Makes explicit that softmax operates on a vector of logits produced by multiple linear classifiers, helping students separate ‘logit computation’ from ‘probability normalization’.
when_to_show: When explaining the pipeline: compute K scores (logits) then apply softmax to get probabilities.
suggested_caption: K linear logits x^T w_k feed into softmax (Wikipedia).

description: Inner product notation x^T w, representing a linear score from features and weights.
teaching_value: Reinforces the basic building block of logits in softmax-based models, useful for interpreting coefficients and implementing the scoring function.
when_to_show: When introducing logits or reviewing how linear layers compute scores before softmax.
suggested_caption: Linear score (logit) computed as x^T w (Wikipedia).

description: Bold vector x, denoting the input feature vector (sample tuple) used in the softmax probability formula.
teaching_value: Clarifies what quantity is being classified and what the linear scores depend on in softmax regression.
when_to_show: When introducing softmax regression notation or explaining x^T w_j as a class score.
suggested_caption: Input feature vector x in softmax regression (Wikipedia).

description: Bold vector w, representing the model’s weight vector(s) used to compute class logits via x^T w_j.
teaching_value: Helps students connect parameters (weights) to class-specific linear functions that feed the softmax.
when_to_show: When explaining how each class j has its own weight vector w_j in multinomial logistic regression/LDA.
suggested_caption: Weight vector w (and class-specific w_j) in softmax models (Wikipedia).

description: The symbol ℝ^K, indicating K-dimensional real space (e.g., a vector of K class scores/logits).
teaching_value: Reinforces the domain/codomain idea: softmax takes a K-dimensional real vector and outputs K probabilities.
when_to_show: When discussing the dimensionality K of the output layer or the input logit vector to softmax.
suggested_caption: ℝ^K: K-dimensional real-valued input to softmax (Wikipedia).

description: Derivative of softmax component σ(q,i) with respect to input q_k: σ(q,i)(δ_ik − σ(q,k)).
teaching_value: Shows the Jacobian structure used in backpropagation and why softmax gradients couple different classes.
when_to_show: When deriving gradients for softmax or explaining why changing one logit affects all probabilities.
suggested_caption: Softmax partial derivative using Kronecker delta (Wikipedia).

description: Indices i, k used to denote softmax output component i and input/logit component k in derivative expressions.
teaching_value: Prevents index confusion when reading or deriving the softmax Jacobian and its symmetry properties.
when_to_show: When students struggle to interpret σ(q,i) vs σ(q,k) or δ_ik in the derivative formula.
suggested_caption: Indices i and k in softmax derivative notation (Wikipedia).

description: Symmetric alternative form of the softmax derivative: ∂/∂q_k σ(q,i) = σ(q,k)(δ_ik − σ(q,i)).
teaching_value: Highlights equivalent gradient forms and supports checking algebra during derivations/backprop implementation.
when_to_show: When presenting multiple equivalent Jacobian entries or validating a student’s gradient derivation.
suggested_caption: Equivalent symmetric form of softmax derivative (Wikipedia).

description: The parameter β, a scaling (inverse-temperature) factor that multiplies logits in a scaled softmax.
teaching_value: Connects scaling to distribution sharpness, preparing students for temperature/entropy control in RL and ML.
when_to_show: When explaining how multiplying logits changes confidence/sharpness or relating β to temperature τ.
suggested_caption: β: scaling (inverse-temperature) parameter for softmax (Wikipedia).

description: Reinforcement-learning softmax (Boltzmann) policy: P_t(a)=exp(q_t(a)/τ)/∑_i exp(q_t(i)/τ).
teaching_value: Demonstrates how action values become a probability distribution and how τ controls exploration.
when_to_show: When introducing stochastic policies in RL or comparing greedy vs softmax/temperature-based exploration.
suggested_caption: Softmax (Boltzmann) action-selection probability in RL (Wikipedia).

description: The term q_t(a), denoting the action-value (expected reward) for action a at time t in RL.
teaching_value: Clarifies what quantity is exponentiated in the RL softmax policy and ties probabilities to value estimates.
when_to_show: When defining symbols in the Boltzmann policy or explaining how higher value increases action probability.
suggested_caption: q_t(a): action value used in RL softmax policy (Wikipedia).

description: The temperature parameter τ used in softmax action selection, controlling how uniform vs peaked probabilities are.
teaching_value: Helps students reason about limiting cases (τ→∞ uniform, τ→0 greedy) and exploration control.
when_to_show: When discussing how to tune exploration in RL or interpreting the effect of temperature on probabilities.
suggested_caption: τ: temperature controlling softness of action probabilities (Wikipedia).

description: Mathematical limit notation showing the temperature parameter approaching infinity: τ → ∞.
teaching_value: Supports explaining softmax exploration in reinforcement learning: high temperature makes action probabilities nearly uniform.
when_to_show: When introducing temperature in softmax policies and discussing the high-temperature (uniform) regime.
suggested_caption: High-temperature limit τ→∞ makes softmax nearly uniform (Wikipedia).

description: Mathematical limit notation showing the temperature parameter approaching zero from above: τ → 0+.
teaching_value: Helps connect low temperature to greedy behavior: softmax concentrates probability on the highest-valued action.
when_to_show: When contrasting exploration (high τ) with exploitation (low τ) and deriving the near-argmax behavior.
suggested_caption: Low-temperature limit τ→0+ makes softmax approach greedy choice (Wikipedia).

description: Big-O notation indicating linear computational complexity in the number of classes: O(K).
teaching_value: Clarifies why standard softmax becomes expensive for large vocabularies: normalization sums over all K outcomes.
when_to_show: When discussing why full softmax is costly in language models or large-class classifiers.
suggested_caption: Standard softmax cost scales linearly: O(K) (Wikipedia).

description: Big-O notation indicating logarithmic computational complexity: O(log2 K).
teaching_value: Supports explaining hierarchical softmax: balanced binary trees reduce computation from linear to logarithmic in K.
when_to_show: When presenting hierarchical softmax as a remedy for large K and comparing complexity to standard softmax.
suggested_caption: Hierarchical softmax can reduce cost to O(log2 K) (Wikipedia).

description: Equation for numerically stable (safe) softmax: σ(z)i = e^{β(zi−m)} / Σj e^{β(zj−m)}.
teaching_value: Shows the standard stability trick (subtract max) to prevent overflow/underflow while preserving probabilities.
when_to_show: When students implement softmax and encounter overflow, or when teaching stable probability computations.
suggested_caption: Safe softmax formula using max-subtraction for stability (Wikipedia).

description: Definition of the stabilizing constant in safe softmax: m = max_i z_i.
teaching_value: Explains exactly what value to subtract in safe softmax and why it bounds exponentials by 1.
when_to_show: Immediately after introducing safe softmax, to specify how to compute the shift term m in code.
suggested_caption: Choose m as the maximum logit: m = max_i z_i (Wikipedia).

description: Single variable symbol q, used as the query vector in attention-style softmax weighting (q^T k_i).
teaching_value: Helps map notation in the attention softmax equation by identifying the query vector symbol.
when_to_show: When unpacking the attention output equation and defining each symbol (q, k_i, v_i).
suggested_caption: q denotes the query vector in attention softmax (Wikipedia).

description: Indexed symbols k1,…,kN representing a set of key vectors.
teaching_value: Supports understanding attention notation: keys are the items compared to the query before softmax normalization.
when_to_show: When introducing the set of keys in attention and explaining the role of N items in the softmax sum.
suggested_caption: k1…kN are the key vectors compared with the query (Wikipedia).

description: Indexed symbols v1,…,vN representing a set of value vectors.
teaching_value: Connects softmax weights to the final weighted sum: values are combined using the normalized attention weights.
when_to_show: When explaining how attention weights produced by softmax are applied to values to form the output.
suggested_caption: v1…vN are value vectors combined by softmax weights (Wikipedia).

description: Equation for attention output using safe softmax weights: o = Σ_i [ e^{q^T k_i − m} / Σ_j e^{q^T k_j − m} ] v_i.
teaching_value: Demonstrates softmax as a normalized weighting mechanism in attention and shows the stability shift m in practice.
when_to_show: When teaching how softmax produces attention weights and how to compute the output vector stably.
suggested_caption: Attention output as a stable softmax-weighted sum of values (Wikipedia).

description: A set of online recurrence equations for attention/softmax computation: z_i = q^T k_i, running max m_i = max(m_{i-1}, z_i), running normalizer ℓ_i = Σ_{t=1..i} exp(z_t − m_i) updated via exp(m_{i-1}−m_i)ℓ_{i-1}+exp(z_i−m_i), and running weighted sum o_i = Σ_{t=1..i} exp(z_t − m_i) v_t updated similarly.
teaching_value: Shows how to compute softmax normalization and the weighted value sum in one pass with numerical stability (max-subtraction), which is the core idea behind online/FlashAttention-style fused attention.
when_to_show: When teaching stable softmax or attention implementation details; use it while walking through a step-by-step streaming computation over keys/values.
suggested_caption: Online stable softmax recurrences for attention (Wikipedia: Softmax function).

description: The final normalized attention output written as the ratio o_N / ℓ_N, where o_N is the accumulated exponentiated-weighted value sum and ℓ_N is the accumulated exponentiated weight sum.
teaching_value: Connects the streaming accumulators to the final softmax-weighted average, clarifying that normalization happens at the end by dividing by the running denominator.
when_to_show: After introducing the online updates for o_i and ℓ_i, to show how to recover the final attention output.
suggested_caption: Final attention output equals accumulated numerator divided by denominator (Wikipedia).

description: Two sequences/vectors of intermediate values: [m_1, …, m_N] for running maxima and [ℓ_1, …, ℓ_N] for running normalization sums across the stream.
teaching_value: Helps students track what state must be maintained across iterations (max and normalizer), reinforcing the minimal memory needed for stable online softmax.
when_to_show: When discussing what to store per step in an online softmax/FlashAttention loop and why these states ensure stability.
suggested_caption: Streaming states: running max m_i and normalizer ℓ_i (Wikipedia).

description: The expression (K−1), referring to the dimension of the standard simplex that softmax outputs lie on.
teaching_value: Supports the geometric interpretation that softmax maps R^K onto a (K−1)-dimensional simplex due to the sum-to-one constraint.
when_to_show: When explaining why softmax outputs have one fewer degrees of freedom than inputs and introducing the simplex view.
suggested_caption: Softmax outputs lie on a (K−1)-simplex (Wikipedia).

description: The vector (x, x, …, x), representing points along the main diagonal in input score space where all logits are equal.
teaching_value: Illustrates the special case of equal logits, preparing students to see why softmax becomes uniform when all inputs match.
when_to_show: When introducing invariances/symmetries of softmax or giving intuition for uniform outputs under equal scores.
suggested_caption: Equal-logit direction: (x, x, …, x) (Wikipedia).

description: The uniform probability vector (1/n, …, 1/n), the softmax output when all logits are equal.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete target output for the equal-logits case, reinforcing that softmax converts equal scores into equal probabilities.
when_to_show: When checking understanding with quick examples (e.g., all logits identical) or validating an implementation.
suggested_caption: Equal logits yield uniform probabilities (1/n,…,1/n) (Wikipedia).

description: A constant shift vector c = (c, …, c), representing adding the same scalar to every logit.
teaching_value: Sets up the translation-invariance property of softmax, which underlies the stable 'subtract max' trick.
when_to_show: Right before proving or using the fact that adding a constant to all logits does not change softmax outputs.
suggested_caption: Constant logit shift vector c = (c,…,c) (Wikipedia).

description: The identity σ(z + c) = σ(z), stating softmax is unchanged by adding the same constant vector to logits.
teaching_value: Directly communicates the key invariance used to improve numerical stability (e.g., subtracting max logit) without changing results.
when_to_show: When motivating safe softmax/log-sum-exp stabilization or explaining why subtracting max preserves probabilities.
suggested_caption: Softmax is invariant to constant shifts: σ(z+c)=σ(z) (Wikipedia).

description: The exponential term e^c, used when factoring out a constant shift from exponentials.
teaching_value: Supports the algebraic proof of softmax shift-invariance by showing the common multiplicative factor introduced by shifting logits.
when_to_show: During the step-by-step derivation that cancels the common factor in numerator and denominator of softmax.
suggested_caption: Common factor from shifting logits: e^c (Wikipedia).

description: The identity e^{z_i + c} = e^{z_i} · e^c, showing how exponentials split over addition.
teaching_value: Enables the proof that a constant shift multiplies all softmax terms by the same factor, which cancels in normalization.
when_to_show: When proving σ(z+c)=σ(z) or explaining why subtracting max improves stability without changing outputs.
suggested_caption: Exponential shift rule used in softmax invariance proof (Wikipedia).

description: A displayed equation showing softmax translation invariance: σ(z+c)_j = e^{z_j+c}/∑_k e^{z_k+c} = (e^{z_j}e^c)/(∑_k e^{z_k}e^c) = σ(z)_j, demonstrating the common factor cancels.
teaching_value: Makes explicit why adding the same constant to all logits does not change probabilities, motivating numerical-stability tricks like subtracting max(logits).
when_to_show: When a learner asks why softmax outputs are unchanged after shifting all scores, or before introducing log-sum-exp stabilization.
suggested_caption: Softmax is invariant to adding a constant to all logits (Wikipedia).

description: A bold vector symbol c indicating a constant-offset vector used for translation: c = (c, …, c).
teaching_value: Clarifies that the shift is applied equally to every coordinate, distinguishing a uniform translation from changing individual logits.
when_to_show: When introducing the notation for shifting logits by the same constant across all classes.
suggested_caption: Constant shift vector c applied to all coordinates (Wikipedia).

description: An equation defining the centering constant as the mean of inputs: c = (1/n)∑ z_i.
teaching_value: Shows a concrete choice of translation (subtracting the mean) to normalize logits onto a sum-zero hyperplane without changing softmax outputs.
when_to_show: When teaching how to normalize logits (e.g., subtract mean) to reduce redundancy before geometric interpretation.
suggested_caption: Choose c as the mean logit to center inputs (Wikipedia).

description: An equation stating the centered-logit constraint: ∑ z_i = 0.
teaching_value: Connects translation invariance to geometry by identifying the (K−1)-dimensional hyperplane representing equivalence classes of logits.
when_to_show: When explaining why softmax effectively removes one degree of freedom and maps from R^K to a (K−1)-simplex.
suggested_caption: Centered logits lie on the hyperplane ∑ z_i = 0 (Wikipedia).

description: An equation stating the simplex constraint on outputs: ∑ σ(z)_i = 1.
teaching_value: Reinforces that softmax outputs form a probability distribution, motivating the simplex/hyperplane geometric picture.
when_to_show: When transitioning from the softmax formula to interpreting outputs as probabilities on the simplex.
suggested_caption: Softmax outputs sum to 1, forming a distribution (Wikipedia).

description: A simple exponential identity: e^0 = 1.
teaching_value: Supports the analogy in the text: centering logits to sum to zero parallels exponentiation mapping 0 to 1 and staying positive.
when_to_show: When a learner needs intuition for why choosing a zero reference is natural before exponentiating logits.
suggested_caption: Exponentiation maps 0 to 1: e^0 = 1 (Wikipedia).

description: A worked 2-class softmax example: σ((0,1)) = (1/(1+e), e/(1+e)).
teaching_value: Provides a concrete numeric form showing how a 1-unit logit difference becomes a specific probability split, useful for intuition and checking calculations.
when_to_show: When introducing softmax with K=2 or comparing softmax to logistic/sigmoid behavior.
suggested_caption: Example: softmax of (0,1) yields (1/(1+e), e/(1+e)) (Wikipedia).

description: A second 2-class example with larger scale: σ((0,2)) = (1/(1+e^2), e^2/(1+e^2)).
teaching_value: Illustrates non-invariance to scaling: increasing the logit gap sharpens the distribution toward the larger logit.
when_to_show: When explaining why multiplying logits changes confidence, or when introducing temperature scaling.
suggested_caption: Example: softmax of (0,2) is more peaked than (0,1) (Wikipedia).

description: A label equation setting the second coordinate: z2 = 0, indicating a chosen reference logit.
teaching_value: Shows how translation invariance lets you fix one logit (or a baseline) to zero to reduce redundancy and simplify analysis in 2D examples.
when_to_show: When simplifying a two-class softmax derivation by setting one logit to zero or discussing baseline classes.
suggested_caption: Fix a reference coordinate: set z2 = 0 (Wikipedia).

description: A label equation setting the first coordinate: z1 = x, parameterizing logits by a single variable when z2 is fixed.
teaching_value: Helps reduce the 2-class case to a one-parameter family, making it easier to relate softmax probabilities to a single logit difference.
when_to_show: When deriving the binary softmax curve by letting z1 vary while holding z2 fixed as a baseline.
suggested_caption: Parameterize logits with one variable: z1 = x (Wikipedia).

description: Equation showing the 2-class softmax component for z1 when z2 is fixed at 0: e^{z1}/(e^{z1}+e^{z2}) = e^{x}/(e^{x}+1).
teaching_value: Connects softmax to the logistic (sigmoid) function, helping students see binary softmax as a special case.
when_to_show: When introducing softmax, to show how it reduces to sigmoid for two classes with one logit set to zero.
suggested_caption: Binary softmax equals sigmoid: e^x/(e^x+1). (Wikipedia)

description: Equation for the second 2-class softmax component when z2=0: e^{z2}/(e^{z1}+e^{z2}) = 1/(e^{x}+1).
teaching_value: Demonstrates that the two softmax outputs in the binary case are complementary and sum to 1.
when_to_show: After presenting the sigmoid form of the first component, to emphasize the second class probability and the sum-to-one constraint.
suggested_caption: Second binary softmax output: 1/(e^x+1). (Wikipedia)

description: Parametrization of the zero-sum line in R^2: (x/2, -x/2), representing logits constrained to sum to zero.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize translation invariance by restricting logits to the sum-zero hyperplane, removing the redundant degree of freedom.
when_to_show: When explaining why adding a constant to all logits does not change softmax, and how to fix a gauge (sum-zero).
suggested_caption: Sum-zero logits line: (x/2, −x/2). (Wikipedia)

description: Equality showing softmax on the sum-zero line matches sigmoid: e^{x/2}/(e^{x/2}+e^{-x/2}) = e^{x}/(e^{x}+1).
teaching_value: Shows that different logit parameterizations (fixing one logit vs. sum-zero) yield the same probability, reinforcing invariance to translation.
when_to_show: When comparing the 'set z2=0' approach with the 'sum logits to zero' approach for binary softmax.
suggested_caption: Sum-zero binary softmax equals sigmoid. (Wikipedia)

description: Complementary equality on the sum-zero line: e^{-x/2}/(e^{x/2}+e^{-x/2}) = 1/(e^{x}+1).
teaching_value: Reinforces that the second class probability is the complement under the sum-zero parametrization, maintaining the sum-to-one property.
when_to_show: Immediately after the first sum-zero identity, to show both class probabilities under the same parametrization.
suggested_caption: Complement on sum-zero line: e^{-x/2}/(e^{x/2}+e^{-x/2}). (Wikipedia)

description: Gradient identity: ∂/∂z_i LSE(z) = exp(z_i)/Σ_j exp(z_j) = σ(z)_i, for i=1..K.
teaching_value: Links softmax to the log-sum-exp function by showing softmax is the gradient of LSE, useful for optimization and backpropagation intuition.
when_to_show: When teaching why softmax appears naturally in convex analysis and how gradients produce normalized probabilities.
suggested_caption: Softmax is the gradient of log-sum-exp. (Wikipedia)

description: Definition of log-sum-exp: LSE(z1,…,zn)=log(exp(z1)+…+exp(zn)).
teaching_value: Provides the core function whose derivative yields softmax, and supports discussions of numerical stability and smooth max approximations.
when_to_show: Before deriving softmax gradients or when introducing stable computation via shifting logits.
suggested_caption: Log-sum-exp definition. (Wikipedia)

description: Jacobian entry for softmax: ∂_{z_j} σ_i = σ_i(δ_{ij} − σ_j).
teaching_value: Gives the exact coupling between outputs in softmax derivatives, crucial for deriving gradients in multiclass cross-entropy training.
when_to_show: When deriving backprop through softmax or explaining why changing one logit affects all class probabilities.
suggested_caption: Softmax Jacobian: ∂σ_i/∂z_j = σ_i(δ_ij−σ_j). (Wikipedia)

description: Symbolic expression V_j(x), denoting the pre-softmax activation (logit/utility) for output j as a function of input x.
teaching_value: Clarifies notation separating logits (V_j) from probabilities (Q_j), helping students map neural network outputs to softmax inputs.
when_to_show: When introducing historical/notation context: define logits V_j(x) before applying the normalized exponential.
suggested_caption: Logit/utility notation V_j(x). (Wikipedia)

description: Softmax probability definition using logits: Q_j(x) = e^{V_j(x)} / Σ_k e^{V_k(x)}.
teaching_value: States the normalized exponential mapping from logits to a valid probability distribution (positive, sums to 1).
when_to_show: When presenting the canonical softmax formula for turning network outputs into class probabilities.
suggested_caption: Softmax: Q_j(x)=e^{V_j(x)}/Σ_k e^{V_k(x)}. (Wikipedia)

description: A rendered equation stating the inverse relationship between inverse temperature and temperature: β = 1/T.
teaching_value: Clarifies the parameterization used in temperature-scaled softmax, helping students translate between β-scaling and T-scaling when interpreting how distributions sharpen or flatten.
when_to_show: Show when a learner is confused about whether to divide logits by T or multiply by β, or when converting between papers/code that use β versus T.
suggested_caption: Inverse temperature definition: β = 1/T (Wikipedia).

description: A rendered equation expressing temperature as the reciprocal of inverse temperature: T = 1/β.
teaching_value: Provides the direct conversion needed to interpret how changing β corresponds to changing T, reinforcing that larger T means smaller β and typically a flatter softmax output.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how increasing temperature affects softmax probabilities, or when a student needs to map a chosen β value to an equivalent temperature.
suggested_caption: Temperature conversion: T = 1/β (Wikipedia).

description: A rendered equation indicating the special case β = 0.
teaching_value: Highlights the limiting case of temperature scaling: β→0 (T→∞) removes preference among logits, motivating why softmax approaches a uniform distribution at very high temperature.
when_to_show: Show when explaining extreme temperatures, especially why very large T (small β) makes outputs nearly uniform regardless of input differences.
suggested_caption: High-temperature limit setup: β = 0 (Wikipedia).

description: A rendered equation computing the base used in a β-parameterized exponential form: b = e^β = e^0 = 1.
teaching_value: Connects β-scaling to an equivalent exponential base; demonstrates that at β=0 the exponential factor becomes 1, so all logits contribute equally, reinforcing the uniform-output intuition.
when_to_show: Show when deriving softmax behavior from exponentials and discussing what happens when the scaling factor goes to zero.
suggested_caption: At β=0, exponential base becomes 1 (Wikipedia).

description: A rendered mathematical expression: softmax(z)^T z, the dot product of the softmax probability vector with the input vector z.
teaching_value: Helps students recognize softmax-weighted averaging/expectation: the expression represents a softmax-weighted sum of components of z, useful for understanding log-sum-exp derivatives and attention-like weighting.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how softmax can be used to compute a weighted average of scores, or when connecting softmax to gradients of log-sum-exp.
suggested_caption: Softmax-weighted dot product: softmax(z)^T z (Wikipedia).

description: Matrix-form projection of an input token embedding matrix X into the query matrix Q using a learned weight matrix W^Q, showing resulting dimensions (sequence length × d_q) and the role of d_model.
teaching_value: Helps students map the attention equations to concrete tensor shapes, clarifying how queries are computed from embeddings before any dot-products or softmax.
when_to_show: Show right after introducing Q, K, V to verify learners can derive Q = XW^Q and track dimensions for their own implementation.
suggested_caption: Computing queries Q = XW^Q (shape tracking). (Benjamin Warner)

description: Self-attention computation in matrix form: Q, K, V matrices; scaled dot-product scores QK^T/√d_k; softmax to produce attention weights; multiplication by V to yield attention output Z, often followed by an output projection W^O.
teaching_value: Provides the full end-to-end attention pipeline in one view, making it easier to connect each mathematical step to code (matmul, scaling, softmax, weighted sum, output projection).
when_to_show: Use as a reference diagram while walking through the forward pass implementation and when debugging mismatched shapes or missing scaling/projection steps.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product self-attention in matrix form. (Jay Alammar)

description: Single-head self-attention schematic showing one attention head producing attention weights over tokens and combining value vectors into a single contextualized output per token (contrasting bidirectional vs causal/cross usage in text).
teaching_value: Helps learners build intuition for what a single head does—computing token-to-token relevance and producing contextualized representations—before adding masking or multiple heads.
when_to_show: Show before introducing causal masking or multi-head attention, to ensure the learner understands the basic per-head computation and outputs.
suggested_caption: Single-head self-attention: weights over tokens produce contextual outputs. (Benjamin Warner)

description: Illustration of scaled dot-product attention producing a softmax attention distribution for a token (e.g., 'it') that places high weight on a relevant prior token (e.g., 'animal'), then forming the weighted sum output.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how scaling and softmax turn similarity scores into interpretable probabilities and how attention can resolve coreference by emphasizing the correct antecedent.
when_to_show: Use when explaining why softmax is applied to QK^T and how attention weights translate into meaningful token associations in language tasks.
suggested_caption: Softmax attention links “it” to “animal” via weighted values. (Benjamin Warner)

description: Two-head attention output depiction showing separate head outputs computed in parallel, then concatenated and projected to return to the model dimension, illustrating how multiple heads attend differently.
teaching_value: Clarifies the mechanics and motivation of multi-head attention: parallel subspaces, distinct attention patterns, concatenation, and final linear projection back to input shape.
when_to_show: Show immediately after learners understand single-head attention, when introducing why multiple heads help and how outputs are combined in code.
suggested_caption: Two-head attention: parallel heads, concat, then project to model dim. (Benjamin Warner)

description: Multi-head attention architecture diagram: input X projected into multiple sets of Q, K, V (one per head), each head computes scaled dot-product attention, head outputs are concatenated, then passed through output projection W^O.
teaching_value: Gives a complete structural blueprint for implementing multi-head attention, emphasizing per-head projections and the concat+projection step that restores d_model.
when_to_show: Use as the primary guide when translating equations into a PyTorch module (reshaping to [batch, heads, seq, head_dim] and back).
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: per-head QKV, concat, output projection. (Benjamin Warner)

description: Causal masking visualization using an upper-triangular mask applied to the attention score matrix so each token can attend only to itself and earlier tokens; masked (future) positions are set to −∞ so softmax yields zero probability.
teaching_value: Makes the causal constraint concrete by showing exactly which score entries are blocked, helping students implement decoder-style attention correctly and avoid information leakage from future tokens.
when_to_show: Show when introducing decoder-only generation or next-token prediction, right before coding the mask addition to attention logits and applying softmax.
suggested_caption: Causal mask blocks attention to future tokens via −∞ logits. (Benjamin Warner)
